| Particle density, $\rho$ ($kg/m^3$) | 2720 |
| Porosity, $n$ | 0.45 |
| Particle radius, $r_p$ (m) | 0.004 |
| Particle effective modulus, $E^*$ (Pa) | $5 \times 10^7$ |
| Normal-to-shear stiffness ratio, $K^*$ | 1.0 |
papers.keyFindings: The study successfully developed a two-stage optimization framework for lunar regolith drilling, identifying that bio-inspired clam-like mechanisms can significantly reduce power consumption. The research determines that specific geometric combinations, such as arc-shaped anchors and sharp cone angles, optimized alongside slow penetration velocities, maximize drilling efficiency in low-gravity environments.

papers.title: Overview of the Lunar In Situ Resource Utilization Techniques for Future Lunar Missions
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a comprehensive review of lunar ISRU technologies, specifically focusing on in situ construction techniques (3D printing, sintering, extrusion), material refining (silicon, metals, fibers), and habitat support systems (life support, energy utilization).
papers.markdownContent:
# Overview of the Lunar In Situ Resource Utilization Techniques for Future Lunar Missions

**Authors:** Peng Zhang, Wei Dai, Ran Niu, Guang Zhang, Guanghui Liu, Xin Liu, Zheng Bo, Zhi Wang, Haibo Zheng, Chengbao Liu, Hanzhe Yang, Yifan Bai, Yang Zhang, Dong Yan, Kefa Zhou, and Ming Gao

**Publication:** *Space: Science & Technology*, 2023;3:Article 0037.
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.34133/space.0037

## Abstract
Building a lunar base by in situ resource utilization (ISRU) will drastically reduce the reliance on supplies from Earth. This article provides a comprehensive review of ISRU technologies, including in situ water access, oxygen production, in situ construction and manufacture, energy utilization, and in situ life support and plant cultivation on the Moon. A 3-step development plan for lunar ISRU technologies in the next decade is proposed.

## Natural Resources on the Moon

### Mineral Resources
Lunar crust minerals are categorized as silicate minerals, oxide minerals, native metals, sulfide minerals, and phosphate minerals. Silicate minerals (pyroxene, plagioclase feldspar, and olivine) make up more than 90% of the volume of most lunar rocks.

### Table 1: Significant lunar mineral modal proportions (volume %)
| Mineral | A | A | A-14 | A-H | A-M | A-16 | A-H | A-M | L-16 | L-20 | L-24 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Plagioclase | 21.4 | 23.2 | 31.8 | 34.1 | 12.9 | 69.1 | 39.3 | 34.1 | 14.2 | 52.1 | 20.9 |
| Pyroxene | 44.9 | 38.2 | 31.9 | 38.0 | 61.1 | 8.5 | 27.7 | 30.1 | 57.3 | 27.0 | 51.6 |
| Olivine | 2.1 | 5.4 | 6.7 | 5.9 | 5.3 | 3.9 | 11.6 | 0.2 | 10.0 | 6.6 | 17.5 |
| Silica | 0.7 | 1.1 | 0.7 | 0.9 | - | 0.0 | 0.1 | - | 0.0 | 0.5 | 1.7 |
| Ilmenite | 6.5 | 2.7 | 1.3 | 0.4 | 0.8 | 0.4 | 3.7 | 12.8 | 1.8 | 0.0 | 1.0 |
| Mare glass | 16.0 | 15.1 | 2.6 | 15.9 | 6.7 | 0.9 | 9.0 | 17.2 | 5.5 | 0.9 | 3.4 |
| Highland glass | 8.3 | 14.2 | 25.0 | 4.8 | 10.9 | 17.1 | 8.5 | 4.7 | 11.2 | 12.8 | 3.8 |

### Water/Ice Resources
Estimated total mass of water in permanently shadowed regions (PSRs) is ~2.9 × 10¹² kg. Infrared sensing indicates pyroclastic deposits may contain up to 1,500 ppm of water.

### Table 2: Average concentrations of solar wind implanted volatiles in lunar regolith
| Volatile | Concentration, ppm (μg/g) | Average mass per m³ (g) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| H | 46 ± 16 | 76 |
| ³He | 0.0042 ± 0.0034 | 0.007 |
| ⁴He | 14.0 ± 11.3 | 23 |
| C | 124 ± 45 | 206 |
| N | 81 ± 37 | 135 |
| F | 70 ± 47 | 116 |
| Cl | 30 ± 20 | 50 |

## In Situ Construction Techniques

### Additive Manufacturing (AM)
1. **Contour Crafting:** Uses liquid-state binders (e.g., sulfur concrete) to build objects layer by layer. NASA explored this for quick infrastructure, though pumping under vacuum remains a challenge.
2. **D-Shape:** Suitable for large-scale construction of protective shells using lunar regolith and liquid ink.

### Sintering Techniques
1. **Microwave Sintering:** Higher efficiency and more even heating than radiant furnaces. Studies on Apollo 17 soil and simulants show improved formability.
2. **Solar Sintering:** Uses concentrated sunlight. Limited depth compared to microwave sintering and takes longer to fabricate.

### Extrusion Printing of Inks
Involves mixing regolith with photocurable resins or polymers. Samples using CLRS-2 simulant and photocurable resins reached average compressive strengths of 428.1 MPa.

## Recommendations and Roadmaps
- **2027:** Mineral resource exploration and usability assessment.
- **2030:** In situ experiments on lunar surfaces using experimental payloads.
- **2035:** Pilot-scale replenishment of vital survival matters for medium-term residence.
- **2040:** Large-scale replenishment for long-term residence and Mars missions.
papers.keyFindings: This review summarizes the current state of lunar ISRU, highlighting that while water extraction from polar PSRs is viable for regional bases, hydrogen reduction of ilmenite offers a globally applicable solution for oxygen/water. It identifies 3D printing (specifically D-Shape and Contour Crafting) and sintering (microwave and solar) as the primary contenders for lunar habitat construction. The study concludes with a 20-year roadmap aiming for large-scale resource replenishment by 2040.
papers.findings: The paper outlines a strategic framework for lunar colonization through sequential ISRU development, focusing on water, oxygen, and construction materials. It emphasizes that the choice of ISRU technology depends heavily on the target location (e.g., polar vs. equatorial) and identifies energy access and mineral beneficiation as the primary technical hurdles.
papers.result: MRE (Molten Regolith Electrolysis) yields approximately 15% to 37.5% oxygen by mass; basalt fibers derived from lunar soil simulants show tensile strengths up to 1,400 MPa; and extrusion-printed regolith-resin composites achieve compressive strengths of 428.1 MPa.

papers.title: Investigation of heat transfer processes in multi-sized solar-sintered regolith for lunar ISRU program
papers.relevanceReason: The paper focuses on solar sintering of lunar regolith for In-situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) and habitat construction, specifically investigating the heat transfer processes and Effective Thermal Conductivity (ETC) of sintered materials.
papers.markdownContent:
# Investigation of heat transfer processes in multi-sized solar-sintered regolith for lunar ISRU program

## Abstract
Effective Thermal Conductivity (ETC) prediction of sintered lunar regolith plays a crucial role in developing extraterrestrial thermal processing and construction techniques for In-situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) projects on the Moon. Herein, we presented a mechanistic model to estimate the ETC of sintered lunar regolith. The Monte-Carlo method was applied to simulate the thermal neck resistance between multi-scaled particles. The model was experimentally validated by the measured ETC and specific surface area of fabricated lunar simulant samples LMS-1. The measured sintered depth was improved by 45% with an optimized particle size distribution (PSD) arrangement.

## 1. Introduction
As space exploration becomes continuously attractive, establishing habitats on extraterrestrial planetary bodies is of particular interest. High-temperature ISRU processes driven by concentrated solar energy have been recognized as a potential pathway to provide materials and power for long-term space explorations and construction on the lunar surface.

### Table 1: Chemical compositions (wt.%) of selected lunar samples and simulants.
| Sample | SiO2 | Al2O3 | CaO | MgO | FeO/Fe2O3 | TiO2 | Na2O | K2O | Others |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apollo | 45.3 | 17.2 | 11.9 | 9.0 | 12.1 | 3.1 | 0.5 | 0.6 | 0.7 |
| Luna | 43.6 | 16.7 | 13.3 | 9.3 | 14.5 | 1.8 | 0.4 | 0.1 | 0.4 |
| Simulant | 46.4 | 15.0 | 9.2 | 7.9 | 12.4 | 2.5 | 3.1 | 1.5 | 1.4 |
| LMS-1 | 46.9 | 12.4 | 7.0 | 16.8 | 8.6 | 3.6 | 1.7 | 0.7 | 1.3 |

## 2. Sintered ETC modeling
The proposed ETC model is built on an improved multi-sized particle system using Monte-Carlo (MC) based algorithms. The heat transferred follows three parallel pathways: solid (Rs), gas (Rg), and radiation (Rrad). Particle shape factors are used to characterize irregular geometry.

### Table 2: Baseline parameters used for ETC modeling.
- Radius of small particle: 15 um
- Radius of medium particle: 47 um
- Radius of large particle: 94 um
- Volume fraction (S/M/L): 0.1/0.7/0.2
- Initial porosity: 0.45
- Sintering temperature: 900–1150 C

## 5. Conclusions
1) An analytical model of sintered LMS-1 thermal conductivity was developed considering the sintered neck resistance between multi-sized particles and unregular shapes. 2) The FVM coupled to the ETC model was established for the heat transfer simulation of the solar sintering system. The temperature overestimation on the top surface can be attributed to the assumption of no ray penetration. The model was further applied to optimize the sintered layer of lunar simulant, and the sintered depth was increased by 45% compared to the original PSD.
papers.keyFindings: The study successfully develops a validated mechanistic model to predict the effective thermal conductivity of solar-sintered lunar regolith. It demonstrates that optimizing particle size distribution can significantly enhance sintering efficiency, increasing the sintered depth by 45% compared to standard simulant distributions.
papers.findings: A mechanistic model using Monte-Carlo simulations accurately predicts the thermal neck resistance and effective thermal conductivity (ETC) of multi-sized sintered regolith. The research identifies that sintering increases thermal conductivity by 5-20 times compared to raw lunar regolith, and that optimized layering of particle sizes improves solar heat penetration and consolidation depth.
papers.result: Experimental validation using LMS-1 simulant showed an average Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 5.5% for steady-state temperature profiles. Optimized particle size distribution increased the measured sintered depth from 18 mm (original PSD) to 26 mm.

papers.title: Development of lunar regolith-based composite for in-situ 3D printing via high-pressure extrusion system
papers.relevanceReason: The paper investigates the development of lunar regolith-based composites and a high-pressure melt extrusion 3D printing system specifically designed for in-situ resource utilization (ISRU) and lunar habitat construction.
papers.markdownContent:
# Development of lunar regolith-based composite for in-situ 3D printing via high-pressure extrusion system

## Abstract
To fully utilize the in-situ resources on the moon to facilitate the establishment of a lunar habitat is significant for long-term residence. This work proposes a cost-effective melting extrusion system for lunar regolith-based composite printing, using engineering thermoplastic powders (PA12) as a bonding agent. High-pressure extrusion enhances interface affinity and maximizes the loading ratio of in-situ resources. Mechanical properties including tensile strength (up to 36.2 MPa) and impact toughness (5.15 kJ/m²) were evaluated. The system allows for an effective loading fraction of up to 80 wt.% and adapts easily to extreme conditions.

## 1. Introduction
Lunar exploration focuses on establishing extraterrestrial habitats. ISRU technologies are preferred to avoid the unaffordable cost of transporting materials from Earth. Lunar regolith is an ideal material for habitat construction due to its thermal and radiation protection properties. While previous AM methods like laser sintering or D-shape printing exist, they often involve high energy consumption or complex auxiliary materials. This study explores melt extrusion molding as a robust, stable, and cost-effective alternative.

## 2. Experimental
### 2.1 Materials
- **Lunar Regolith Simulant:** Basalt powder with particle size distribution (mean 101.084 µm) similar to Apollo samples.
- **Binder:** PA12 thermoplastic polymer (nylon).
- **Composites:** PA12-Basalt mixtures at 40, 50, and 60 wt.% basalt loading.

### 2.2 Table 1: Material Composition (wt.%)
| Sample | SiO2 | TiO2 | Al2O3 | FeO | MgO | CaO | Na2O | K2O | Others |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Apollo 14 | 48.10 | 1.70 | 17.40 | 10.40 | 9.40 | 10.70 | 0.70 | 0.55 | 1.05 |
| CE-5 | 42.20 | 5.00 | 10.80 | 22.50 | 6.48 | 11.00 | 0.26 | 0.19 | 1.57 |
| Basalt Simulant | 54.48 | 1.39 | 14.89 | 8.66 | 6.31 | 7.16 | 2.71 | 0.39 | 4.01 |

### 2.4 Specimens Fabrication
Two types of extruders were used:
1. **Piston-based pellet extruder:** Used for small-batch material performance testing.
2. **Screw-based pellet extruder:** Developed for robot-assisted large-scale component fabrication.

## 3. Results and Discussion
- **Thermal Stability:** TGA/DSC tests showed that PA12 remains stable below 350°C. Basalt particles promote the recrystallization of PA12, which is beneficial for extrusion molding.
- **Mechanical Performance:** Tensile strength decreases as basalt weight fraction increases (due to interface cracking), while elastic modulus increases (basalt acts as a hard reinforcement phase).
- **Impact Resistance:** Crucial for meteorite impact resistance. Maximum toughness (5.15 kJ/m²) was achieved at 60 wt.% basalt loading using a 45°/−45° infill pattern.
- **Large-Scale Printing:** A lunar habitat architectural model and honeycomb structures were successfully printed with loading up to 80 wt.% basalt.

### Table 6: Selected Mechanical Properties
| Weight fraction (wt.%) | Temp (°C) | Speed (mm/s) | σb (MPa) | E (MPa) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 40 | 225 | 7 | 36.162 | 1674.18 |
| 50 | 225 | 7 | 23.055 | 1634.31 |
| 60 | 225 | 7 | 19.500 | 2443.41 |
| 40 | 225 | 8 | 32.392 | 1467.06 |

## 4. Conclusions
The study demonstrates that high-pressure melt extrusion molding is an effective and energy-efficient approach for lunar ISRU construction. It achieves significantly higher regolith loading (up to 80 wt.%) than many alternative methods while maintaining structural integrity and impact resistance suitable for lunar environments.
papers.keyFindings: The research successfully developed a high-pressure melt extrusion 3D printing system capable of using lunar regolith-based composites with up to 80 wt.% loading. The resulting material demonstrated strong mechanical performance, reaching a maximum tensile strength of 36.2 MPa and significant impact toughness to resist meteorite impacts. This method is highlighted as more cost-effective and energy-efficient than laser-based sintering for lunar habitat construction.
papers.findings: High-pressure melt extrusion provides a viable path for Class III habitat construction using polymer-bonded lunar regolith. The addition of basalt simulant acts as a reinforcement phase that increases the elastic modulus and impact toughness of the composite, though it reduces overall tensile strength compared to pure polymer. The use of a screw-based extruder mounted on a robotic arm enables the fabrication of complex, large-scale structures like honeycomb grids and habitat models.
papers.result: The maximum tensile strength reached 36.2 MPa at 40 wt.% basalt loading (7 mm/s printing speed). The maximum impact toughness reached 5.15 kJ/m² at 60 wt.% basalt loading. Successful printing was achieved with regolith loading as high as 80 wt.%.

papers.title: SUITABILITY OF SILICONE FOR SOFT-ROBOTIC EXPLORATION OF TERRESTRIAL AND EXTRATERRESTRIAL OCEAN WORLDS
papers.relevanceReason: The paper explores the suitability of silicone rubber (Ecoflex) for constructing soft-robotic explorers and sealing electronics in extreme extraterrestrial environments like Europa, Titan, and Enceladus. It specifically addresses material properties, extreme temperature resistance, and potential for additive manufacturing through re-bonding.
papers.markdownContent: # SUITABILITY OF SILICONE FOR SOFT-ROBOTIC EXPLORATION OF TERRESTRIAL AND EXTRATERRESTRIAL OCEAN WORLDS  **Authors:** A. Nuncio Zuniga, W. Fink   **Affiliation:** Visual and Autonomous Exploration Systems Research Laboratory, University of Arizona  **Journal:** Space Science and Technology (Космічна наука і технологія), 2023, Vol. 29, No. 3, pp. 47–56  **DOI:** 10.15407/knit2023.03.047  ## ABSTRACT This work revisits mechanical and chemical properties of silicone rubber — Ecoflex — to assess its suitability for soft-robotic explorer construction and as a sealant for electronics in extreme planetary liquid environments, such as Earth’s oceans and extraterrestrial ocean worlds (Europa, Titan, Enceladus). Tests included strain under various temperatures, re-bonding properties for additive manufacturing, and resistance to dissolution in salt water and hydrocarbons. Results indicate Ecoflex is an effective non-conducting sealant and pliable material for temperatures above -50 °C.  ## 1. INTRODUCTION Exploration of subsurface oceans on moons like Europa and Enceladus requires flexible explorers capable of navigating small apertures. This paper investigates silicone rubber for constructing these soft-robotic explorers and sealing communication beacons.  ## 2. MATERIALS AND METHODS ### 2.1 Ecoflex Overview Ecoflex 00-30 (Smooth-On, Inc.) was used. It is a proprietary silicone rubber mixed in equal parts (A and B). It cures in 4 hours at room temperature and is known for sub-zero temperature resistance and high elasticity.  ### 2.2 Strain Testing Prisms (1.25 x 1.00 x 7.50 cm) were cast at three mixing ratios (A:B): 20:80, 50:50, and 80:20. Testing was conducted at -5 to 0 °C, ~22.4 °C, and 39 to 40 °C.  ### 2.4 Very Low Temperature Testing Samples were tested at -7.5 °C, -20 °C, -80 °C, and -196 °C (liquid nitrogen) to observe freezing and thawing behavior.  ### 2.5 Re-bonding Ability Cured samples were joined with fresh liquid Ecoflex to test the potential for additive manufacturing and repairs.  ## 4. RESULTS ### 4.1 Strain Performance Increased amounts of compound A resulted in a stiffer material. Strain results were largely temperature-independent within the measured range above freezing.  ### 4.2 Stretching to Failure The 50:50 mix reached a breaking point at an elongation of 553.3 ± 20.5%.  ### 4.3 Low Temperature Results Samples did not freeze at -20 °C but froze at -80 °C. Freezing was fully reversible; samples returned to pre-freezing pliability once thawed. At -196 °C, samples shattered but regained pliability upon returning to room temperature.  ### 4.4 Re-bonding The interface between old and new cured Ecoflex was nearly indistinguishable and did not break under stress, supporting additive manufacturing applications.  ### 4.5 Chemical Resistance Cured Ecoflex showed no degradation after 21 days in salt water or 2 months in mineral oil (hydrocarbon stand-in).  ## 5. CONCLUSIONS Ecoflex 00-30 is an excellent candidate for soft-robotic construction and electronic encapsulation for missions to subsurface oceans. It maintains pliability down to -20 °C (likely -50 °C) and is chemically resistant to aqueous and hydrocarbon environments.
papers.keyFindings: Ecoflex silicone rubber maintains its mechanical integrity and pliability down to -50°C, making it suitable for subsurface ocean exploration on icy moons. It demonstrates high chemical resistance to both saline and hydrocarbon liquids and possesses strong re-bonding capabilities essential for additive manufacturing and electronic encapsulation.
papers.findings: The research establishes silicone rubber as a viable material for extreme extraterrestrial environments. It highlights the material's temperature-independent elasticity above its glass transition point and its excellent performance as an electrical insulator and hermetic sealant in saline and hydrocarbon-rich environments.
papers.result: Ecoflex 00-30 achieved a 553.3 ± 20.5% elongation at break and maintained hermetic sealing of electronics for 21 days in saturated salt water. It successfully demonstrated re-bonding with material aged up to 6 days, and reversible freezing behavior at -80°C.

papers.title: An assumption of in situ resource utilization for “bio-bricks” in space exploration
papers.relevanceReason: The paper reviews Microbially Induced Carbonate Precipitation (MICP) as a technique for in situ resource utilization (ISRU) to manufacture 'bio-bricks' from lunar regolith for extraterrestrial human settlements.
papers.markdownContent:
# An assumption of in situ resource utilization for “bio-bricks” in space exploration

**Authors:** Hongyan Zuo, Shuisong Ni, and Meiying Xu
**Journal:** Frontiers in Materials
**Type:** Mini Review
**Published:** 06 April 2023
**DOI:** 10.3389/fmats.2023.1155643

## Abstract
Microbially induced carbonate precipitation (MICP) mimics the natural cementation process that occurs in various geological settings by using the bicarbonate minerals resulting from various bacterial metabolic pathways as cementing agents. This bio-technique can be used to manufacture so-called “bio-bricks,” which rival regular bricks in strength and durability. This process has also been shown to have great potential as an energy-saving and cost-effective means of in situ resource utilization (ISRU) to produce construction materials for extraterrestrial human settlements such as lunar exploration. We review the metabolic activities leading to calcium carbonate precipitation, properties of lunar regolith, and the production of bio-bricks.

## 1 Introduction
Extraterrestrial settlements require ISRU due to the high cost of transporting material from Earth (~$10,000/lb to orbit). Traditional processing methods like melting/sintering are energy-intensive. Bio-cementation (MICP) is recognized as a green solution, using microorganisms to induce carbonate mineral precipitation for construction.

## 2 Microbial carbonate precipitation by different metabolic activities
Metabolic pathways for MICP include:
- **Aerobic:** Photosynthesis, Ureolysis (most studied), and Ammonification.
- **Anaerobic:** Sulfate reduction, Denitrification, and Methane oxidation.

### 2.1 Aerobic processes
Ureolysis is the enzymatic degradation of urea by ureases, producing carbonic acids and ammonia, which increases pH and dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC) to induce precipitation. *Sporosarcina pasteurii* (formerly *Bacillus pasteurii*) is a primary model organism due to its high levels of secreted urease.

### 2.2 Anaerobic processes
Given the lack of oxygen on the moon, anaerobic processes like sulfate reduction and denitrification are critical. Denitrification has been evaluated as a suitable MICP method for soil reinforcement due to substrate solubility and carbonate yield.

## 3 Properties of sand-sized material on the moon
Lunar regolith is fragmental and unconsolidated rock material. The average mean particle size (D50) is approximately 72 μm, with a specific gravity of 2.9–3.5 g/cm³. Major mineral phases include plagioclase, pyroxene, ilmenite, and olivine. Lunar soil contains Ca, which can serve as a source for carbonate precipitation.

## 4 Characteristics of artificial bio-bricks
Previous studies on bio-bricks (Table 2) show they can achieve unconfined compressive strength (UCS) comparable to conventional cement-treated materials. 

### Table 2: From sand to artificially “bio-bricks” and comparable UCS
| Material | D50 (mm) | Mineralogy | Microbe | UCS (MPa) | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Masonry sand | 0.42 | Quartz | S. pasteurii | 1.0–2.2 | Bernardi et al. (2014) |
| Graded silica sand | 0.42 | - | S. pasteurii | ~9 | Cheng et al. (2020) |
| Ottawa silica sand | 0.51 | 99% quartz | S. pasteurii | ~3.5 | Li et al. (2020a) |
| Silica sand | - | - | S. pasteurii | 5–23 | Nething et al. (2020) |
| Standard sand | 0.73 | 98% silica | S. pasteurii | 1.08–7 | Zhao et al. (2020) |

## 5 Research needs and future directions
- **Particle Size:** Lunar regolith (D50 = 72 μm) is smaller than the sands typically used in lab bio-brick experiments (0.36–0.73 mm).
- **Survival:** Microbes must be selected to survive extreme lunar conditions (gravity, radiation, temperature).
- **Resources:** Human urine could serve as a source of urea. Water ice in permanently shaded regions could save costs.
- **Cell-free systems:** Urease enzyme systems might bypass the challenges of culturing live microbes in situ.
papers.keyFindings: Microbially induced carbonate precipitation (MICP) offers an energy-efficient alternative to sintering for producing lunar construction materials ('bio-bricks'). While most research uses *Sporosarcina pasteurii* and aerobic ureolysis, anaerobic metabolic pathways like denitrification are more suitable for the oxygen-deprived lunar environment.
papers.findings: The review identifies that MICP can cement various sand types into bricks with structural integrity. It highlights that the mineralogy and grain size of lunar regolith are generally compatible with MICP processes, though the smaller grain size of regolith compared to terrestrial lab sands requires further investigation. The study also emphasizes the potential of utilizing local resources like water ice and human waste (urine for urea) to support the bio-brick production process.
papers.result: Bio-bricks produced via MICP have shown unconfined compressive strengths (UCS) ranging from 1.0 MPa to 23 MPa, depending on the microbial species, nutrient concentration, and sand characteristics. This range makes them comparable to conventional cement-treated beams used in terrestrial construction.

papers.title: Envisioning Extraterrestrial Construction and the Future Construction Workforce: A Collective Perspective
papers.relevanceReason: The paper directly addresses extraterrestrial construction, the role of robotics and additive manufacturing, the necessity of ISRU for habitat construction on the Moon and Mars, and the specific technological needs for the future construction workforce in extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# Envisioning Extraterrestrial Construction and the Future Construction Workforce: A Collective Perspective

## Abstract
Humanity’s evolution toward an interplanetary species poses a new frontier for the construction industry: extraterrestrial construction. With fast technological advancements in manufacturing and robotics, it is a matter of when, not if, before humans make perpetual habitats on nearby planetary bodies. This paper summarizes the outcomes of a national interdisciplinary workshop structured around three core themes: future work (extraterrestrial construction), future workers (extraterrestrial workforce), and future technology (emerging technologies for workforce training).

## 1 Introduction
NASA and ESA reveal plans to establish permanent human habitats on the Moon and Mars by 2040. Extraterrestrial construction involves building anthropogenic environments in outer space. Conventional processes are ill-suited for vacuum, radiation, and low gravity. Feasible exploration requires in-situ construction (ISRU) and automated construction processes.

## 2 Methodology
A two-day virtual interdisciplinary workshop was conducted involving 26 experts from engineering, architecture, robotics, and planetary geoscience to identify needs and challenges.

## 4 Summary of Outcomes
### 4.1 Theme 1: Extraterrestrial Construction
1. **Site Conditions:** Differences in gravity, radiation, vacuum, and abrasive regolith must be clearly determined.
2. **Protocols:** Conventional tasks change; e.g., excavation forces dislodge massive objects more easily in low gravity.
3. **ISRU:** In-situ Resource Utilization is a necessity due to Earth-to-space delivery costs.
4. **Complexity:** Requires a technical taxonomy to avoid errors like the Mars Climate Orbiter unit conversion disaster.
5. **Human-in-the-loop:** Robots require human intervention for repairs, oversight, and creative problem-solving.
6. **Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC):** Essential for phases ranging from landing pads to enclosed habitats.

### 4.2 Theme 2: Extraterrestrial Construction Workforce
Workers need situational awareness, geological knowledge, and cross-disciplinary skills. Training must address teleoperation latency (up to 20 mins for Mars), lack of GNSS, and isolated medical support.

### 4.3 Theme 3: Emerging Technologies for Workforce Training
AI-XR (Extended Reality) environments are recommended to simulate low gravity, lighting conditions, and sensory deprivation. Digital twin systems and NASA task load index (TLX) metrics are useful for evaluation.

## 5 Conclusions and Takeaways
Extraterrestrial construction will be robot-intensive but human-supervised. Training must transition from traditional classroom settings to immersive, learner-centered constructivist environments using AI and XR technologies.
papers.keyFindings: Extraterrestrial construction is fundamentally robot-intensive but necessitates a 'human-in-the-loop' model for maintenance, troubleshooting, and high-level oversight. Training for this workforce must shift to AI-XR immersive environments to effectively simulate the hazards of low gravity, radiation, and communication latency.
papers.findings: The paper identifies that extraterrestrial construction protocols must differ significantly from terrestrial ones due to site-specific variables like low gravity and abrasive regolith. It emphasizes that In-Situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) is a logistical necessity and that successful construction will depend on advanced Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC).
papers.result: The study establishes a framework for extraterrestrial construction based on three themes: Future Work (ISRU and automation), Future Workers (multi-skilled teleoperators), and Future Technology (AI-XR training platforms).

papers.title: Fiber-reinforced lunar geopolymers synthesized using lunar regolith simulants
papers.relevanceReason: The paper investigates the synthesis and mechanical characterization of fiber-reinforced geopolymers using lunar regolith simulants (LHS-1 and LMS-1) and local fibers (basalt and human hair) specifically for lunar ISRU and additive construction purposes.
papers.markdownContent:
# Fiber-reinforced lunar geopolymers synthesized using lunar regolith simulants

**Journal:** Acta Astronautica 214 (2024) 593–608  
**Authors:** Solomon Debbarma, Xijun Shi, Anthony Torres, Mehrab Nodehi  
**Published:** 11 November 2023 (Elsevier Ltd.)

## Abstract
In-situ resource utilization (ISRU) is critical for lunar construction. This study formulates fiber-reinforced geopolymers using two lunar regolith simulants: Lunar Mare Simulant (LMS-1) and Lunar Highlands Simulant (LHS-1). The effects of simulant type, curing temperature, and fiber reinforcement (basalt fiber and human hair) on flowability, compressive strength, and flexural strength were investigated. Results indicate LHS-1 geopolymers exhibit higher strength. Curing at 93 °C and 5% RH for 72 h significantly developed strength in LHS-1. Basalt and human hair fibers improved flexural strength by up to 32% and 15% respectively, acting as nucleation sites and structural bridges.

## 1. Introduction
Conventional cement is impractical for the Moon due to the lack of limestone and high sintering temperatures (1450 °C). Geopolymers, which are alkali-activated materials rich in Si and Al, offer a viable alternative. Lunar regolith is rich in these elements, making it suitable for geopolymerization. This research aims to support NASA's goals for ISRU and extreme environment construction.

## 2. Materials and Methods
- **Simulants:** LMS-1 (mare) and LHS-1 (highlands) from Exolith Labs.
- **Fibers:** Basalt fibers (BF) (12 mm length) and Human hair fibers (HHF) (up to 15 mm).
- **Activator:** Reagent sodium silicate (40% Na4O4Si, 60% H2O).
- **Curing Regimes:** Ambient (20 °C, 50% RH), High-temperature (93 °C/99.6 °C, 5% RH), and low-temperature conditioning (−30 °C).

## 3. Results and Discussion

### 3.1. Flowability
LHS-1 (Mix B) showed higher flow (85 mm) than LMS-1 (Mix A, 74 mm). A 50:50 mix (Mix D) achieved the highest flow (94 mm). Fiber addition reduced flowability by 9–26%.

### 3.2. Compressive Strength
LHS-1 geopolymer achieved ~31.26 MPa under heat-curing (93 °C), while LMS-1 only reached ~8.24 MPa. The higher calcium content and lower Si/Al ratio in LHS-1 contributed to better strength development. Low-temperature exposure (−30 °C) slightly reduced strength but the material remained stable.

### 3.3. Flexural Strength and Fibers
Adding BF (0.4–1.2% vol) improved flexural strength by up to 32% (3.07 MPa for Mix G). HHF (1.5% vol) improved it by 15% (2.67 MPa). Excess fiber (1.5% BF) caused clumping and reduced strength.

### 3.4. Microstructure (SEM/EDS)
SEM analysis confirmed that fibers act as nucleation sites for geopolymerization products and bridge cracks, enhancing ductility. EDS confirmed the formation of N–A–S–H and (C–N)-A–S–H gels.

## 4. Conclusions
- LHS-1 geopolymers are superior to LMS-1 for structural applications.
- High-temperature curing (lunar day simulation) is essential for geopolymer hardening.
- Basalt and human hair fibers are effective reinforcements that can be sourced in situ.
- The materials meet the theoretical minimum strength requirements for lunar gravity (1/6 of Earth's).

## Table 4: Mix Proportions (Selected Data)
| Mix | LMS-1 % | LHS-1 % | Fiber Type | Dosage (vol %) | Curing (°C) |
|-----|---------|---------|------------|----------------|-------------|
| A   | 100     | -       | -          | -              | 20/93       |
| B   | -       | 100     | -          | -              | 20/93       |
| D   | 50      | 50      | -          | -              | 99.6        |
| G   | 50      | 50      | BF         | 1.2            | 99.6        |
| I   | 50      | 50      | HHF        | 1.5            | 99.6        |
papers.keyFindings: LHS-1 lunar highlands simulant produces geopolymers with significantly higher compressive strength (31.26 MPa) than LMS-1 mare simulant when heat-cured at 93 °C. The addition of basalt and human hair fibers enhances flexural strength by up to 32% through crack bridging and nucleation effects, though it slightly decreases compressive strength due to increased porosity.
papers.findings: The study demonstrates the feasibility of creating geopolymer binders from lunar regolith simulants, emphasizing that chemical composition (particularly calcium content and Si/Al ratios) and curing environment are the primary drivers of mechanical performance. Highlands-based geopolymers performed better than mare-based ones, and high-temperature curing mimicking the lunar day was found necessary for optimal strength development.
papers.result: Maximum compressive strength reached 31.26 MPa for heat-cured LHS-1; maximum flexural strength improvement of 32% (reaching 3.07 MPa) was achieved using 1.2% basalt fiber reinforcement in a 50:50 simulant blend.

papers.title: Building a lunar base with 3D printing
papers.relevanceReason: The document details a collaborative study by ESA and Foster + Partners into the feasibility of 3D printing lunar habitats using ISRU materials (lunar regolith simulant) and binder jetting technology.
papers.markdownContent:
# Building a lunar base with 3D printing

**January 31, 2013**

(Phys.org)—Setting up a lunar base could be made much simpler by using a 3D printer to build it from local materials. Industrial partners including renowned architects Foster + Partners have joined with ESA to test the feasibility of 3D printing using lunar soil.

"Terrestrial 3D printing technology has produced entire structures," said Laurent Pambaguian, heading the project for ESA. "Our industrial team investigated if it could similarly be employed to build a lunar habitat."

Foster + Partners devised a weight-bearing 'catenary' dome design with a cellular structured wall to shield against micrometeoroids and space radiation, incorporating a pressurised inflatable to shelter astronauts. A hollow closed-cell structure – reminiscent of bird bones – provides a good combination of strength and weight.

The base's design was guided in turn by the properties of 3D-printed lunar soil, with a 1.5 tonne building block produced as a demonstration.

### Construction Process
Once assembled, the inflated domes are covered with a layer of 3D-printed lunar regolith by robots to help protect the occupants against space radiation and micrometeoroids.

"3D printing offers a potential means of facilitating lunar settlement with reduced logistics from Earth," added Scott Hovland of ESA's human spaceflight team.

The UK’s Monolite supplied the D-Shape printer for ESA's 3D-printed lunar base study, with a mobile printing array of nozzles on a 6 m frame to spray a binding solution onto a sand-like building material. 3D ‘printouts’ are built up layer by layer.

**Material and Mechanism:**
- **Simulated Material:** Lunar material mixed with magnesium oxide to turn it into 'paper'.
- **Structural 'Ink':** A binding salt is applied which converts the material to a stone-like solid.
- **Current Speed:** 2 m per hour.
- **Next-generation Speed:** 3.5 m per hour (capable of completing a building in a week).

### Environmental Challenges
Italian space research firm Alta SpA worked with the Scuola Superiore Sant'Anna on adapting techniques to a Moon mission and ensuring process quality control. 

**Vacuum Conditions:** Liquids typically boil away in a vacuum. The team discovered that by inserting the 3D printer nozzle 2 mm beneath the regolith layer, capillary forces keep the droplets trapped, allowing the process to function in a vacuum.

**Simulant Source:** Basaltic rock from a volcano in central Italy was found to have a 99.8% resemblance to lunar soil.

**Future Considerations:**
- Control of hazardous lunar dust.
- Thermal factors (3D printing works best at room temperature, while lunar temperatures fluctuate wildly).
- Settlement location: Lunar poles offer the most moderate temperature ranges.
papers.keyFindings: ESA and its partners demonstrated the feasibility of 3D printing lunar structural elements using binder jetting technology and regolith simulant. They successfully produced a 1.5-tonne block and identified that sub-surface binder injection allows the process to function in a vacuum environment.
papers.findings: The study suggests a hybrid construction approach using Class II inflatables for pressure and Class III 3D-printed shells for environmental protection. It highlights that the D-Shape printing process can use magnesium oxide and binding salts to solidify regolith at a rate of 2-3.5 meters per hour.
papers.result: Production of a 1.5 tonne building block; identification of a basaltic simulant with 99.8% lunar soil resemblance; verification of capillary-force binding in vacuum at 2mm depth.

papers.title: Designing sustainable built environments for Mars habitation: Integrating innovations in architecture, systems, and human well-being
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a comprehensive review of Mars habitation, specifically covering in situ resource utilization (ISRU) for construction materials like basalt and regolith, 3D printing technologies for module fabrication, and structural design strategies for extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# Designing sustainable built environments for Mars habitation: Integrating innovations in architecture, systems, and human well-being

**Authors:** Hongli Sun, Mengfan Duan, Yifan Wu, Yunyi Zeng, Hengxin Zhao, Shuangdui Wu, Borong Lin, Ronggui Yang, and Gang Tan
**Journal:** Nexus
**Date:** September 17, 2024
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ynexs.2024.100030

## Summary
Migration to Mars and building livable habitation gradually become prosperous and arduous roads for human progress. Although many technical breakthroughs have been achieved for supporting migration, sustainable survival on Mars needs further attention. The Mars habitation module serves as humans' first barrier from the harsh extraterrestrial conditions that people will spend almost 100% of time in the module. Thus, the indoor physical environment plays an important role in long-term and even permanent living on Mars.

## Introduction
Mars dwelling involves two key aspects: resource sustainability and human sustainability, encompassing both the efficient construction and operation of Mars habitation and the healthy survival of humans. In situ resources on Mars are utilized for module construction through novel 3D printing technology, which is also expected to protect humans from space radiation risk and heavy ions.

### Comparison of Environments (Table 1 Summary)
| Feature | Earth | ISS | Moon | Mars |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gravity | 1 g | None | 1/6 g | 1/3 g |
| Atmosphere | 78% N2, 21% O2 | Artificial | None | Rarefied CO2 (95.3%) |
| In situ resources | Wide varieties | None | SiO2, Al2O3, CaO | Martian soil, Basalt, Volcanic ash |
| Energy Sources | Solar, Wind, Tidal | Solar | Solar, Helium-3 | Solar, Radioactive isotope |

## Mars Habitation Technologies
### Design of Mars Habitation Module
*   **Module Form:** Above-ground (e.g., Mars Ice House), semi-underground, and underground (lava tubes) modules are proposed to mitigate radiation and storms.
*   **Materials (ISRU):** Martian basalt is a primary candidate due to radiation resistance and high specific heat. Martian soil (containing sulfur) can be developed into sulfur-based concrete. Magnetites mixed with elemental sulfur have shown a compressive strength of 4.62 kgf/mm².
*   **Construction:** 3D printing offers possibilities for utilizing in situ materials. Examples include robotic systems like Team Zopherus and high-temperature sintering for producing "Planetary LEGO Bricks."

## Operation and Well-being
*   **Thermal Control:** High-insulation materials (aerogels), heat pipes, and phase change materials are used to maintain stable internal temperatures despite external fluctuations (-120°C to -60°C).
*   **Life Support:** Regenerative systems for oxygen generation (electrolyzed water) and CO2 scrubbing are critical. 
*   **Human Centricity:** Rhythmic artificial lighting is proposed to align with human circadian rhythms (24h 37min Martian day).

## Findings and Results
*   **Energy Efficiency:** Parametric architectural design can reduce energy consumption by up to 15.8% compared to traditional structural design.
*   **Material Performance:** Basalt fiber insulation technologies have thermal conductivities as low as 0.031–0.038 W/(m·K).
*   **Light Transmission:** Single-glazed BIPV windows can achieve a daily conversion efficiency of up to 6.0% and visible light transmittance of 11%.

## Conclusion
Achieving a sustainable Mars habitat requires a multidisciplinary integration of architecture, material science, and life support systems, moving from "technology-centered" to "human-centered" performance design.
papers.keyFindings: The paper emphasizes the transition from technology-centered to performance-centered design in Mars habitation, integrating ISRU materials with automated construction. Key innovations include 3D-printed basalt structures for radiation shielding and AI-driven environmental controls for human well-being.
papers.findings: The review identifies ISRU-based 3D printing (using basalt and sulfur concrete) as the primary pathway for scalable Mars construction. It also highlights the necessity of coupling habitat structural design with operational performance systems (thermal, lighting, air) to ensure long-term sustainability and human health.
papers.result: Identified Martian basalt-based rock wool as a high-performance insulator (0.031–0.038 W/m·K) and determined that parametric generative design can optimize building forms to reduce operational energy consumption by 15.8%.

papers.title: 3D printed sulfur-regolith concrete performance evaluation for waterless extraterrestrial robotic construction
papers.relevanceReason: The paper investigates 3D printing using sulfur-regolith concrete (SRC), which is a waterless ISRU-based material for lunar and martian habitat construction, focusing on automated construction and material performance in vacuum conditions.
papers.markdownContent:
# 3D printed sulfur-regolith concrete performance evaluation for waterless extraterrestrial robotic construction

**Journal:** Automation in Construction 165 (2024) 105571  
**Authors:** Ilerioluwa Giwa, Mary Dempsey, Michael Fiske, Ali Kazemian  
**Available online:** 18 June 2024

## Abstract
By leveraging the capabilities of construction 3D printing, building structures in harsh extraterrestrial environments is conceivable. Sulfur concrete is a waterless construction material that offers great potential to replace Portland cement concrete (PCC) in extraterrestrial construction. The shape stability of 3D printed Martian sulfur regolith concrete (SRC) was found to benefit from a lower substrate layer temperature. However, this comes at the cost of flexural strength, resulting in up to 53% strength loss. The printed SRC specimens demonstrated a significantly faster strength development rate (gaining about 85% of the ultimate strength after only 12 h) compared to the printed PCC. The printed SRC specimens also outperformed the PCC specimens in vacuum conditions at higher temperatures. Furthermore, modifying the SRC materials with Dicyclopentadiene resulted in up to 44% strength increase and minimized the sublimation rate of the printed specimens in vacuum, especially at an elevated temperature.

## 1. Introduction
To promote successful exploration efforts and enable a long-term presence on the Moon and Mars, supporting infrastructure such as landing and launch pads, habitats, hangers, research labs, and protective shields are needed. Given these inhospitable conditions and the limited available human workforce, conventional construction methods that rely on manual efforts cannot be adopted for extraterrestrial construction. Extrusion-based Construction 3D Printing (C3DP) holds significant potential for construction in such harsh and perilous conditions, owing to its robotic and autonomous capabilities.

## 3. Methodology
### 3.1. Materials and mixture proportions
In this study, the SRC materials were prepared using a commercially available elemental sulfur powder (99.9% purity) as the binder, and Mars Global Simulant (MGS-1) as the fine aggregate. Based on preliminary trials, a sulfur content of 33% (by weight) was selected.

| Oxides | SiO2 | TiO2 | Al2O3 | FeO | MnO | MgO | CaO | Na2O | K2O | P2O5 | LOI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wt. (%) | 42.9 | 0.6 | 12.8 | 11.2 | 0.1 | 14.6 | 7.4 | 1.5 | 0.6 | 0.1 | 5.5 |
*Table 1: Chemical composition of the MGS-1 regolith simulant.*

| Mixture ID | Sulfur (kg/m³) | Regolith (kg/m³) | DCPD (kg/m³) | Cement (kg/m³) | Sand (kg/m³) | Limestone Powder (kg/m³) | Water (kg/m³) | HRWRA (%) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SRC | 872 | 1744 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| MSRC | 799 | 1596 | 80 | — | — | — | — | — |
| PCC | — | — | — | 510 | 1464 | 76 | 234 | 0.36 |
*Table 2: Mixture Proportions for the SRC, MSRC, and PCC printing materials.*

## 4. Results and Discussion
- **Fresh-state properties:** The average wet density of SRC was 2.310 g/cm³ compared to 2.096 g/cm³ for PCC. Flowability and slump of SRC increased with material temperature.
- **Shape Stability:** Layer deformations solely occurred at the shortest interlayer time gap (45 s). At 130 °C and 150 °C, deformations were 10% and 23%, respectively. Longer gaps (120 s, 180 s) showed 0% deformation.
- **Mechanical Performance:** Printed SRC specimens gained ~85% of ultimate strength after only 12 h. In contrast, PCC specimens only achieved 19% of their 28-day strength in the same period.
- **Vacuum Conditions:** SRC specimens showed negligible weight loss at 25 °C in vacuum. At 55 °C, weight loss increased to 0.92% due to sublimation. MSRC (modified with 10% DCPD) showed an 85% decrease in weight loss compared to unmodified SRC at 55 °C.

## 5. Conclusions
- Martian SRC was successfully 3D printed, proving the feasibility of waterless off-world additive construction.
- Geometrical accuracy and flexural strength are influenced by extrusion temperature and substrate layer temperature.
- SRC specimens exhibited higher compressive and flexural strength than PCC counterparts in both mold-cast and printed formats.
- SRC is highly recyclable; specimens were remelted and reprinted without losing strength.
- Modification with 10% DCPD enhanced compressive strength by up to 44% and significantly reduced the sulfur sublimation rate.
papers.keyFindings: The study demonstrates that 3D printed Martian sulfur-regolith concrete (SRC) is a viable waterless alternative to cement, exhibiting rapid strength development (85% in 12 hours) and high recyclability. While shape stability is better at lower substrate temperatures, it comes with a flexural strength penalty, and modification with Dicyclopentadiene (DCPD) is crucial for improving mechanical properties and reducing sublimation in vacuum environments.
papers.findings: Waterless sulfur-regolith concrete is suitable for extrusion-based 3D printing in lunar and martian environments. Its performance depends heavily on temperature management during the printing process to balance shape stability against interlayer bonding. It outperforms traditional Portland cement concrete in terms of rapid strength gain and stability in vacuum, especially when chemical modifiers are used.
papers.result: DCPD modification of SRC resulted in a 44% increase in compressive strength and a 34% increase in flexural strength, while reducing vacuum weight loss by 85% at 55°C compared to unmodified SRC.

papers.title: Designing sustainable built environments for Mars habitation: Integrating innovations in architecture, systems, and human well-being
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a comprehensive review of Mars habitation construction, focusing on ISRU materials (basalt, sulfur concrete), additive manufacturing (3D printing), robotic construction, and structural designs for surface and underground environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# Designing sustainable built environments for Mars habitation: Integrating innovations in architecture, systems, and human well-being

**Authors:** Hongli Sun, Mengfan Duan, Yifan Wu, Yunyi Zeng, Hengxin Zhao, Shuangdui Wu, Borong Lin, Ronggui Yang, Gang Tan  
**Journal:** Nexus (CellPress)  
**Date:** September 17, 2024  
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ynexs.2024.100030

## Summary
This review offers a perspective on developing Mars habitation modules from design to operation. It highlights the multidisciplinary challenges of combining resource sustainability with human health and well-being in the harsh Martian environment.

## Environmental Characteristics Comparison (Table 1 Summary)
| Feature | Earth | ISS | Moon | Mars |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Atmosphere | 78% N2, 21% O2 | Artificial | None | Rarefied CO2 (95.3%) |
| Temperature | -90 to 50°C | -270°C | -190 to +137°C | -120 to -60°C |
| Gravity | 1 g | None | 1/6 g | 1/3 g |
| Pressure | 101.3 kPa | None | 3 x 10^-13 kPa | 0.6 kPa |
| In situ resources | Wide varieties | None | SiO2, Al2O3, CaO | Martian soil, Basalt, Volcanic ash |

## Key Habitation Technologies

### Habitat Construction
- **Materials:** Abundant Martian basalt is a primary candidate for construction due to radiation resistance and high specific heat. Sulfur-based concrete is highlighted for its ease of operation and fast solidification in dry/cold environments. 
- **Techniques:** 
  - **3D Printing:** Utilizing in situ regolith for module construction to protect against radiation.
  - **Robotic Construction:** Automated assembly systems (e.g., 'Chinese Super Mason') to assemble bricks produced by 3D printing.
  - **Structural Forms:** Above-ground (inflatable cores with shells), semi-underground, and underground (utilizing lava tubes to mitigate radiation and thermal risks).

### Habitat Operation
- **Thermal Control:** Use of high-insulation materials (aerogels, vacuum panels), heat pipes, and phase change materials.
- **Air Support:** Electrolyzed water and CO2-to-Oxygen conversion via solid oxide electrolysis or microwave-plasma technology.
- **Energy:** Nuclear energy for consistent output; PV panels (8-13.5% efficiency on Mars); and vortex-induced vibration wind generators.

### Human Well-being
- **Indoor Environment:** Dynamic bionic control of temperature and lighting (biodynamic lighting) to maintain circadian rhythms and psychological health.
papers.keyFindings: Mars habitation requires a shift from technology-centered engineering to human-centered design that integrates architecture with life-support systems. In situ resource utilization (ISRU), particularly using basalt and sulfur-based concrete through autonomous 3D printing and robotic assembly, is the most viable path for construction.
papers.findings: The review establishes a systematic 'design to operation' framework for Mars habitats. It identifies that while structural engineering is maturing, the integration of performance-centered building envelopes (using materials like silica aerogel and basalt fiber) and automated environmental control systems is critical for long-term psychological and physiological human survival.
papers.result: Basalt fiber thermal conductivity is reported at 0.031–0.038 W/(m·K). Mars solar radiation is approximately 587 W/m² (0.43 times Earth's). Sulfur-based concrete is noted for suitability on Mars due to lack of water requirement and fast curing. Performance-centered parametric design can reduce indoor energy consumption by approximately 15.8%.

papers.title: Exploring Multi-Directional 3D Printing for Enclosures on Earth and Beyond: Sensitivity Analysis of Buildability
papers.relevanceReason: The paper investigates multi-directional 3D concrete printing (3DCP) techniques specifically for constructing extraterrestrial habitats on Mars and the Moon, focusing on the buildability of enclosures without formwork.
papers.markdownContent:
# Exploring Multi-Directional 3D Printing for Enclosures on Earth and Beyond: Sensitivity Analysis of Buildability

**Authors:** Nusrat Tabassum, Ph.D. and José Pinto Duarte, Ph.D.
**Conference:** Earth and Space 2024
**Publisher:** ASCE
**DOI:** 10.1061/9780784485736.073

## Abstract
Architecture in space demands innovative solutions for constructing robust, airtight habitats. Additive manufacturing (AM) has emerged as a promising technology for habitats on celestial bodies like Mars. While 3D printing on Earth is effective for walls, challenges persist for roof construction and enclosures. This study explores multi-directional printing using combined horizontal (corbelling) and inclined slicing techniques to lower printable overhang angles (traditionally limited to 60 degrees). A sensitivity analysis using a structural simulator predicts the number of printable layers before collapse. Results suggest it is possible to lower overhang angles while maintaining buildability, providing a step toward design optimization for 3D printed enclosures.

## Introduction
3D printing in space habitat construction addresses challenges in harsh environments, as seen in NASA's 3D-Printed Habitat Challenge. Current 3D Concrete Printing (3DCP) techniques often require a vertical nozzle, limiting overhang angles to approximately 60°. This necessitates pointed arch geometries to avoid formwork. This study explores the hypothesis that combining different slicing techniques can lower these overhang angles, increasing design flexibility for space structures.

## Methodology
The research follows a four-stage workflow:
1.  **Form Generation and Decomposition:** Using shape grammar to break down enclosures into printable patches.
2.  **Sensitivity Analysis:** Using a structural behavior simulator (Duarte et al. 2023) to understand the impact of geometric variables on buildability.
3.  **Transition Strategies:** Determining how to switch between slicing techniques.
4.  **Optimization and Toolpath Generation.**

### Geometric Variables for Sensitivity Analysis
- **V1:** Overhang angle (Constant)
- **V4:** Wall height (Independent)
- **V5:** Room length (Independent)
- **V7:** Wall thickness / Number of filaments (Independent)
- **V8:** Number of printable layers (Dependent)

## Results
Simulation outcomes for vault configurations at 60°, 45°, and 30° printing angles indicate:
-   **30° Angle:** Yielded the highest level of relative printability (91% of the necessary layers for a full vault were successfully simulated).
-   **Room Length:** Longer room lengths generally increase interlayer printing time and potential for more layers, up to a limit (approx. 3m length for 1m wall height).
-   **Wall Thickness:** Increased thickness (more filaments) directly correlates to a higher number of printable layers.
-   **Maximum Layers:** For the tested configurations, the highest number of printable layers achieved were 134 (at 60°), 132 (at 45°), and 132 (at 30°).

## Findings
1. It is feasible to combine corbelling and inclined slicing to achieve overhang angles as low as 45° and 30° without structural collapse during printing.
2. Trade-offs for maximizing printable layers include decreasing wall height while increasing room length, or increasing room length while decreasing the printing angle.
3. Wall thickness is a critical factor in preventing premature structural failure during the additive process.

## Conclusion
This sensitivity analysis demonstrates how geometric variables influence the buildability of 3D printed enclosures. It serves as a foundation for developing design optimization tools to assist in the creation of formwork-free structures for space applications. Future work will involve physical validation through test prints and analysis of collapse modes.
papers.keyFindings: The research demonstrates that combining corbelling and inclined slicing techniques in 3D concrete printing allows for stable construction of enclosures with overhang angles as low as 30 degrees without formwork. Sensitivity analysis reveals that structural buildability is maximized by optimizing the trade-offs between room length, wall height, and filament count (thickness).
papers.findings: A systematic sensitivity analysis was performed using a structural simulator to evaluate the impact of geometry on 3DCP buildability. The study found that while traditional vertical nozzle printing is limited to 60° overhangs, multi-directional printing enables lower angles. Key factors affecting stability include the wall thickness (number of filaments) and the relationship between room length and wall height, which influences the interlayer cooling/setting time and cumulative stress.
papers.result: The 30° printing angle configuration achieved the highest relative printability, reaching 91% of the required vault layers (132 of 146 layers). For specific test cases with a 0.5m wall height and 4m room length, the simulator predicted stability up to 134 layers for 60° and 132 layers for 45°/30° angles.

papers.title: Bearing capacity of lunar soil ground under extraterrestrial environmental effects
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides fundamental geotechnical data on the bearing capacity of lunar regolith, which is critical for foundation design of any surface-based habitat (Class I, II, or III) and explores the mechanical properties of soil in extraterrestrial conditions.
papers.markdownContent:
# Bearing capacity of lunar soil ground under extraterrestrial environmental effects

**Banglu Xi** (a,b), **Mingjing Jiang** (c), **Pinqiang Mo** (b), **Jiaqiang Yang** (a), **Zhenhua Zhang** (a)

(a) Collage of Civil Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China
(b) State Key Laboratory for GeoMechanics and Deep Underground Engineering, China University of Mining & Technology, Xuzhou 22100, China
(c) School of Civil Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 2150092, China

**Journal:** Computers and Geotechnics 165 (2024) 105923
**Received:** 9 August 2023; **Accepted:** 7 November 2023

### Abstract
Lunar environment features a low gravity field and ultra-high vacuum which shows significant effects on the bearing behavior of lunar soil ground for future lunar habitation. Therefore, the 2D Discrete Element Method (DEM) was employed to simulate the plate load tests under different gravity fields in non-vacuum and high-vacuum environments. The results show that a bilinear line bounded by 1g can be employed to describe the gravity effect on factors for ultimate bearing capacity and coefficient of subgrade reaction. High-gravity effects in a centrifuge cannot be directly applied to predict low-gravity lunar soil behavior. The affected area becomes deeper and wider with decreasing gravity level in high-vacuum environments.

### 1. Introduction
Lunar habitation plays an important role in the future of human society as a supply center of in-situ resources. Design of suitable foundations requires accurate understanding of lunar soil-tool interaction. The lunar environment's low gravity and ultra-high vacuum (leading to van der Waals forces between particles) present challenges for Earth-based geotechnical modeling. This study uses DEM to analyze these extraterrestrial effects on bearing behavior.

### 2. DEM Model of Lunar Regolith
The model captures apparent cohesion and high peak friction angles using a rolling resistance (M) formulation:

- M = (Kn ̢ ̢ r^2 / 12) ̢ ̢ ̢, if M < KnUn̢r / 6
- Fv = A̢r / (6̢ ̢ D^3)

**Table 1: Parameters for lunar soil bin in DEM simulations.**
| Name | Value |
|---|---|
| Void ratio | 0.22 |
| Dry density (kg/m3) | 2600 |
| Normal stiffness (N/m) | 7.5 x 10^7 |
| Tangential stiffness (N/m) | 5.0 x 10^7 |
| Frictional coefficient | 1.0 |
| Rolling resistance coefficient | 1.3 |
| Thickness of absorbed molecular layer (m) | 1.5 x 10^-8 |
| Damping coefficient (kg/s) | 0.4 |

### 3. Bearing Behavior Analysis
- **Ultimate Bearing Capacity (qu):** Determined at s = 2.5 mm. Increases non-linearly with increasing gravity. High-vacuum conditions result in higher bearing capacity than non-vacuum due to van der Waals forces enhancing shear strength.
- **Bilinear Gravity Effect:** A bilinear fitting curve bounded by 1g is proposed for gravity factors. The slopes differ in low and high gravity regimes, suggesting centrifuge testing (high-g) does not directly translate to lunar conditions (low-g).
- **Subgrade Reaction (K):** Increases with gravity level. Coefficients are larger in high-vacuum environments than non-vacuum at identical gravity levels.

### 4. Soil Response and Failure Modes
- **Heaving:** Ground heaving increases with settlement and is more pronounced in high-vacuum environments.
- **Affected Area:** In high-vacuum (lunar-like), as gravity decreases, the failure zone becomes deeper and wider. In non-vacuum, it becomes deeper but narrower.

### 5. Conclusions
1. A bilinear relationship describes the gravity effect on bearing capacity factors.
2. Van der Waals forces in ultra-high vacuum are non-ignorable in low gravity fields.
3. The affected area and failure mechanisms vary significantly between vacuum and non-vacuum conditions, providing a basis for new analytical models for lunar foundation design.
papers.keyFindings: Bearing capacity of lunar soil follows a bilinear relationship with gravity level, meaning centrifuge data cannot be extrapolated to lunar gravity directly. The high vacuum environment significantly increases cohesion and bearing capacity due to van der Waals forces, altering failure mechanisms.
papers.findings: The study utilized 2D Discrete Element Method (DEM) to simulate plate load tests under varying gravity and vacuum levels. It discovered that vacuum conditions create deeper and wider failure zones in low gravity compared to Earth-like conditions, necessitating specific foundation design strategies for lunar habitats.
papers.result: Simulated peak friction angles for lunar regolith remain constant at approximately 43 degrees, but apparent cohesion increases from 0.36 kPa to 2.2 kPa depending on stress. Gravity factors (sng-qu) were established for low-gravity (slope ~0.58) and high-gravity (slope ~0.23) regimes.

papers.title: The first biological experiment on lunar surface for Humankind: Device and results
papers.relevanceReason: The paper details the design and results of the first biological experiment on the lunar surface, focusing on a Bioregenerative Life Support System (BLSS). This is critical for long-term space habitat sustainability, life support integration, and identifies future research needs in ISRU and lunar construction materials.
papers.markdownContent:
# The first biological experiment on lunar surface for Humankind: Device and results

**Authors:** GengXin Xie, JingHang Ding, YuanXun Zhang, MaoZhi Ren, Dan Qiu, Xi Wang
**Journal:** Acta Astronautica 214 (2024) 216–223
**Published:** October 2023 (Online), 2024 (Volume)

## Abstract
The Bioregenerative Life Support System (BLSS) is an indispensable part of extraterrestrial bases. The Chinese Chang’e 4 lunar lander carried a micro-ecosystem called Biological Experiment Payload (BEP) to the back of the Moon to assess the effects of low gravity, intense radiation, and light on living things. The payload (2.608 kg) contained cotton, potato, Arabidopsis, and rape seeds, plus fly eggs and yeast. Results showed that cotton seeds germinated faster on the Moon (22 h) than on Earth (53 h) and demonstrated that plants could survive extreme temperature drops during the lunar night if properly managed.

## 1. Introduction
Building a lunar base requires self-sufficiency due to high transportation costs. BLSS, using producers, consumers, and decomposers, is a key technology for food, oxygen, and water supply. While facilities like BIOS-3 and Biosphere-2 have tested these on Earth, the effects of 1/6 gravity and lunar radiation remained uncertain.

## 2. The Biological Experiment Payload (BEP)
### 2.1. Specifications
- **Weight:** 2.608 kg
- **Dimensions:** 173 mm (Diameter) × 198 mm (Height)
- **Inside Space:** 0.82 L total; 0.42 L bio-activity space.
- **Structure:** Cylindrical aluminum shell with multilayer insulation and thermoelectric coolers (TEC).
- **Components:** Water tank (18 ml), biological chamber with vermiculite substrate, light guide tube, two CCD cameras, and control chips.

### 2.2. Biological Material
- **Producers:** Cotton, Potato, Arabidopsis, Rape.
- **Consumer:** Drosophila (Fly eggs).
- **Decomposer:** Saccharomyces (Yeast).

## 3. Ground Testing and Reliability
- **Sealing:** Air leakage rate < 1.9 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·cm²/s.
- **Thermal:** Simulated ranges from -52 °C to 52 °C (passive) or 38 °C (active cooling).
- **Radiation:** Tested tolerance to X-rays 10⁴~10⁶ times lunar intensity; most organisms survived except fly eggs at highest doses.

## 4. Lunar Surface Results
- **Landing:** January 3, 2019, Von Kármán crater.
- **Germination:** Cotton seed germinated 22 h after watering (vs. 53 h on Earth).
- **Morphology:** Lunar cotton seedlings had curved stems, fat streamlined nodes close to the substrate, and curled leaves.
- **Environmental Data:** Internal temperature 16.7–36.5 °C during day; dropped to -52 °C during night. Internal pressure: 98–104 kPa.
- **Mission Duration:** Worked for 1300 h over five lunar days; decommissioned May 9, 2019.

## 5. Discussion and ISRU Implications
The experiment proved plant cultivation on the Moon is possible. The authors note that future research should focus on:
- Improving lunar soil for cultivation.
- Utilizing lunar rocks as construction materials.
- Using lunar lava tubes for base construction.
- Producing oxygen and water from lunar minerals.

## Tables
| Lunar Day | Working Times | Photos | Temp Range (°C) | Pressure Range (kPa) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | 212.75 h | 169 | 16.7–37.5 | 98–104 |
| 2nd | 276.85 h | 120 | 16.7–37.5 | 91–100 |
| 3rd | 286.05 h | 129 | 16.7–37.5 | 88.2–99.2 |
| 4th | 291.92 h | 120 | 16.7–49.5 | 85.7–96.1 |
| 5th | 219.27 h | 93 | 16.7–49.5 | 82.3–94.5 |
papers.keyFindings: The BEP experiment on Chang'e 4 achieved the first plant germination (cotton) on the lunar surface, revealing that 1/6 gravity and natural lunar sunlight support biological growth but alter plant morphology. Seedlings demonstrated unexpected resilience, remaining green after surviving a lunar night at -52°C, which suggests potential for low-energy life support systems in future habitats.
papers.findings: Plants can germinate and initiate growth on the lunar surface using natural sunlight and closed-loop environmental controls. The study identifies morphological differences in growth under 1/6 gravity and highlights the necessity of integrated ISRU research for future sustainable lunar bases.
papers.result: Cotton seeds germinated in 22 hours on the Moon compared to 53 hours in Earth controls; the payload maintained internal atmospheric pressure between 82.3 and 104 kPa and temperatures between -52°C and 49.5°C over a 1300-hour mission duration.

papers.title: Silicate-lunar regolith composite: A vacuum self-hardened and comprehensively durable extraterrestrial construction material
papers.relevanceReason: The paper introduces a Silicate-Lunar Regolith Composite (SLRC) specifically for lunar and Martian construction, focusing on ISRU, materials science, and vacuum-based self-hardening mechanisms suitable for extreme space environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# Silicate-lunar regolith composite: A vacuum self-hardened and comprehensively durable extraterrestrial construction material

**Journal:** Construction and Building Materials 449 (2024) 138517  
**Authors:** Zifan Geng, Lizhi Zhang, Zhiwen Wu, Jiale Huang, Xiangyu Wang, Ming Jen Tan, Wei She, Hao Zhou, Guoqing Geng

## Abstract
Extraterrestrial exploration, represented by Lunar base construction, has been promoted worldwide as a multi-disciplinary, cutting-edge and strategic issue. Herein we proposed a novel extraterrestrial construction material composed of Lunar/Martian regolith simulants and only 4 wt% of silicate adhesive. This silicate-regolith composite can be directly self-hardened in vacuum within 4.5 h without any curing to achieve 29 MPa of hardening strength. Meanwhile, it shows comprehensive durability with stable microstructures and >70 % of residual strength against long-term vacuum exposure, huge temperature difference and intense γ-ray and proton radiations. Its in-situ solidification and durability originate from vacuum dehydration, where silica clusters in solution spontaneously cohere into a solid network with strong connections of silica tetrahedra and bound water. A moderate silicate modulus around 2.5 and appropriate modifications can disperse finer silica clusters to increase oligomer silica units in solution and enhance hardening strength. Compared with lunar geopolymer and other existing extraterrestrial construction materials, this vacuum self-hardening composite allows a cryogenic preparation even below −40 °C using diverse regolith with a wide range of compositions and sizes, proposing a facile, durable and versatile solution towards in-situ extraterrestrial constructions.

## 1. Introduction
Infrastructure on the Moon faces rigorous environments: temperature differences (-200 °C to 120 °C), high vacuum (10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻⁹ Pa), radiation, moonquakes, and meteoroid impacts. Transportation costs make ISRU (In-Situ Resource Utilization) essential. Existing materials like sintered regolith (low strength, high energy), polymers/sulfur (durability issues), or geopolymers (requiring sealed heat curing) have significant drawbacks.

## 2. Materials and Methods
### 2.1. Materials
- **Lunar Regolith Simulants (LRS):** LRS-1 (Apollo-17 simulant), LRS-2 (Apollo-14), LRS-3 (Apollo-12).
- **Martian Regolith Simulant (MRS):** Spirit-355 simulant.
- **Binders:** Sodium silicate (SS) with adjusted modulus (2.0, 2.5, 3.0) and modifying agents (sodium pyrophosphate, sodium dodecyl sulfate, hydrothermal treatment).

### 2.2. Sample Preparation
1. Regolith and silicate solution mixed (Mass ratio 0.14:1).
2. Cold-pressed at 9.5 MPa into 40x10x10 mm³ green bodies.
3. Exposed to high vacuum (<10 Pa).
4. Simulated lunar environment: Temperature cycling between -196 °C and 78 °C.

## 3. Results and Discussion
### 3.1. Self-hardening and Durability
- **Hardening:** Rapid water loss in vacuum leads to self-hardening within 4.5 h.
- **Strength:** SS2.5 modulus produced optimal results (Flexural: 5.48 MPa, Compressive: 18.19 MPa). Modified binders (SS2.5HT) reached flexural strength of 6.54 MPa and compressive strength of 20.34 MPa within 4.5 h.
- **Long-term performance:** After 4 lunar cycles (108 days), SLRC reserved 71-81% of flexural strength and 88-98% of compressive strength compared to initial hardening.

### 3.2. Microstructure
SEM analysis shows regolith particles covered with membranous silicate binders bridging the particles. Porosity remains stable around 34.7 vol% (hardening stage) to 37.4 vol% (after first midday), becoming invariant after the first lunar cycle.

## 4. Comparison with Other Materials

| Item | Material compositions | Processing Temp. | Compressive Strength | Durability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Selective Sintering | Regolith only | 1000–1100 °C | 2–5 MPa | Uncertain |
| Bulk Sintering | Regolith only | 1000–1100 °C | 2–152 MPa | Uncertain |
| Lunar Geopolymer | Regolith + 6–32% alkali | 60–195 °C | 16–50 MPa | Uncertain |
| Thermoset Polymer | Regolith + 18–43% resin | 20–80 °C | 90–156 MPa | >95% (thermal) |
| **Silicate-regolith** | **Regolith + <4% binder** | **Flexible (≥-40 °C)** | **20–29 MPa** | **72–100% stable** |

## 5. Conclusion
The Silicate-Lunar Regolith Composite (SLRC) offers a unique vacuum self-hardening mechanism that requires no external heat, making it suitable for cryogenic regions (poles) or sunless lava tubes. It utilizes low binder percentages (~4 wt%) and maintains high durability against radiation and extreme thermal cycles.
papers.keyFindings: This study develops a Silicate-Lunar Regolith Composite (SLRC) that achieves a 29 MPa hardening strength in vacuum within 4.5 hours using only 4 wt% silicate binder. The material requires no external heating (effective down to -40 °C) and demonstrates high durability, retaining over 70% of its strength after exposure to simulated extreme lunar conditions, including radiation and temperature cycling from -196 °C to 78 °C.
papers.findings: Discovery of a vacuum-driven self-hardening mechanism for regolith-silicate composites that bypasses the need for thermal curing. The process is mediated by vacuum dehydration where silica clusters cohere into a solid network, providing stable adhesion even in cryogenic conditions.
papers.result: The SLRC achieved 20-29 MPa compressive strength; 71-81% flexural strength retention and 88-98% compressive strength retention after 4 months of simulated lunar environment cycling; successful hardening achieved at temperatures as low as -40 °C.

papers.title: Silicate-lunar regolith composite: A vacuum self-hardened and comprehensively durable extraterrestrial construction material
papers.relevanceReason: The paper discusses a novel construction material (Silicate-Lunar Regolith Composite) specifically designed for in-situ lunar and Martian construction using vacuum self-hardening, which is highly relevant to ISRU and automated construction in extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent: # Silicate-lunar regolith composite: A vacuum self-hardened and comprehensively durable extraterrestrial construction material ## Abstract Extraterrestrial exploration, represented by Lunar base construction, has been promoted worldwide. Herein we proposed a novel extraterrestrial construction material composed of Lunar/Martian regolith simulants and only 4 wt% of silicate adhesive. This silicate-regolith composite can be directly self-hardened in vacuum within 4.5 h without any curing to achieve 29 MPa of hardening strength. It shows durability against long-term vacuum exposure, temperature differences (-200 to 120 C), and radiation. ## 1. Introduction Establishing a lunar habitat is supported by NASA (Artemis) and ESA projects. Infrastructure faces rigorous environments: temperature differences, vacuum (10^-10 Pa), and radiation. Novel construction materials are needed to utilize lunar resources (ISRU) and minimize transport costs. ## 2. Materials and methods ### 2.1. Materials Lunar regolith simulants (LRS-1, LRS-2, LRS-3) and Martian regolith simulant (MRS) were used. Silicate solution (sodium silicate) with modulus 2.0, 2.5, and 3.0 was employed. ### 2.2. Sample preparation Mixing regolith and 4 wt% silicate binder, cold-pressing at 9.5 MPa, and vacuum dehydration (<10 Pa). Testing environments mimicked lunar diurnal cycles (-196 to 78 C). ## 3. Results and discussion ### 3.1. Self-hardening and durability SLRC rapidly loses water and self-hardens in vacuum. SS2.5 generates 5.48 MPa flexural and 18.19 MPa compressive strength. Modified binders (SS2.5HT) achieve 20.34 MPa compressive strength within 4.5 h. Over long-term (108 days), SLRC retains 71-81% of flexural and 88-98% of compressive strength. ### 3.4. Comprehensive evaluation SLRC consumes minimal carried materials (<=4%) compared to geopolymers (6-32%) or sulfur concrete (25-40%). It allows cryogenic preparation below -40 C, unlike geopolymers requiring >60 C curing. ## 4. Conclusion The material satisfies requirements for in-situ extraterrestrial construction, especially for polar regions and lava tubes. It simplifies logistics by requiring only a single-component binder and no thermal curing.
papers.keyFindings: The researchers developed a Silicate-Lunar Regolith Composite (SLRC) that hardens in vacuum through dehydration rather than thermal curing, using only 4% carried binder. The material achieves high compressive strength (up to 29 MPa) and maintains structural integrity under extreme lunar temperature cycles and radiation.
papers.findings: The study identifies vacuum dehydration as a viable hardening mechanism for silicate-based regolith composites, eliminating the need for energy-intensive thermal curing. The process is effective across various regolith compositions (Lunar and Martian) and particle sizes, demonstrating robustness for in-situ resource utilization.
papers.result: The composite achieved 29 MPa hardening strength in vacuum within 4.5 hours and maintained over 70% residual strength after 108 days of simulated lunar environmental exposure. It successfully solidified at temperatures as low as -40 degrees Celsius, representing a breakthrough for construction in permanently shadowed regions.

papers.title: Additive manufacturing evolution in construction: From individual terrestrial to collective, aerial, and extraterrestrial applications
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides an extensive review of additive manufacturing (AM) for construction in extreme environments, specifically addressing extraterrestrial applications on the Moon and Mars, the use of regolith as an in-situ resource (ISRU), and autonomous robotic construction systems.
papers.markdownContent:
# Additive manufacturing evolution in construction: From individual terrestrial to collective, aerial, and extraterrestrial applications

**Journal:** Journal of Building Engineering 96 (2024) 110389  
**Authors:** Kamran Aghaee, Linfei Li, Alireza Roshan, Parsa Namakiaraghi  
**Published:** 10 August 2024

## Abstract
