This study reviews the advancement of Additive Manufacturing (AM) in the construction industry, tracing its evolution from individual terrestrial to collective, aerial, and extraterrestrial construction. It elaborates on bio-inspired technology, topology optimization, preferred materials for extraterrestrial construction, and the challenges associated with AM on lunar surfaces.

## 1. Introduction
Automation in construction via AM can expedite timelines, enhance safety, and contribute to economic efficiency. Recent trajectories involve moving from off-site to on-site construction, upscaling, and autonomous remote operations.

## 4. Advanced AM techniques (terrestrial and non-terrestrial)
### 4.1. Collective robotic construction (CRC)
Involves a coordinated group of autonomous robots operating simultaneously to enhance and expedite the construction of structures at a larger scale and lower cost.

### 4.2. Aerial AM
UAV-based AM (drones) allows access to elevated and challenging environments without scaffolding. Challenges include limited payload, drone stability, and battery life.

### 4.3. Lunar AM
- **Objective:** Establish habitats on the Moon or Mars before 2040.
- **Key Players:** NASA, ESA, ICON, AI SpaceFactory (Marsha habitat).
- **ISRU:** Use of local materials like lunar soil and Martian dust (regolith).
- **Martian Concrete:** 'Marscrete' is primarily composed of sulfur and regolith.
- **Ice Construction:** 'Mars Ice House' utilizes H2O as a resource.

## 5. AM construction considerations and characteristics
### 5.1. a.m. concrete materials
 Extraterrestrial cementitious materials exhibit superior compressive and flexural strength compared to conventional Portland cement when utilizing locally sourced regolith.

| Planet | Temperature (°C) | Oxygen (%) | Relative humidity (%) | Gravity (m/s²) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Moon | −171–127 | 45 | ~0 | 1.625 |
| Mars | −143–17 | 0.13 | 80-100 (night); ~0 (day) | 3.721 |
| Earth | −89.3–56.7 | 21 | 0–100 | 9.807 |

## 9. Summary and Outlook
Extraterrestrial AM is promising due to its automation, efficiency, and resource utilization. Benefiting from laser technology (melting regolith) and autonomous robotics (CRC) can expedite construction timelines essential for establishing infrastructure with limited resources.
papers.keyFindings: Additive Manufacturing (AM) is evolving towards autonomous collective and aerial robotic systems for extreme environments, specifically for lunar and Martian surfaces. The study highlights that in-situ resource utilization (ISRU) using regolith for materials like 'Marscrete' (sulfur-based) or laser-melted soil is essential for sustainable Class III habitat construction.
papers.findings: The paper reviews the full trajectory of construction AM, identifying collective robotics and ISRU as the primary pillars for extraterrestrial infrastructure. It discusses technical requirements for extraterrestrial concrete, including rheology control, durability under extreme temperature fluctuations (-171 to 127 °C), and the impacts of partial gravity on structural design.
papers.result: NASA goals are to establish habitats on the Moon/Mars by 2040; ICON was awarded a $57.2 million contract for lunar infrastructure; Marscrete (sulfur/regolith) and Mars Ice House (H2O based) are identified as key ISRU material strategies; lunar gravity (1.625 m/s²) necessitates different load-bearing calculations compared to Earth.

papers.title: In-situ additive manufacturing with lunar regolith for lunar base construction: A review
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a comprehensive review of in-situ additive manufacturing (AM) technologies using lunar regolith (ISRU) for lunar base construction, covering materials science, geotechnical properties, and various automated construction methods suitable for extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# In-situ additive manufacturing with lunar regolith for lunar base construction: A review

## Abstract
Construction of lunar bases is critical to further space exploration and decrease dependency on Earth-based resources. Abundant energy, water, and minerals on the Moon favor it as an ideal candidate for building extraterrestrial habitats. This paper discusses the feasibility of using in-situ resource utilization (ISRU) of lunar regolith (LR) for additively manufactured technologies for a lunar base. This work synthesizes current research in an overview at the intellectual level for the state-of-the-art methodologies of lunar regolith utilization in additive manufacturing (AM) processes. It makes a comparison of various techniques of AM, several kinds of materials, their maturity, and unique challenges of the lunar environment: extreme temperatures and microgravity.

## 1. Introduction
The Moon, as the closest celestial body to Earth, has drawn significant interest due to its diverse resources. Dwelling building modules are more complex and require more functions and structural shapes than space stations. NASA, ESA, and CNSA have all conducted work towards these goals. However, due to the uniqueness of the lunar environment, existing technologies are not yet sophisticated enough. The challenge of transporting related materials from Earth remains unresolved.

## 3. In-situ material: Lunar regolith (LR) and lunar regolith simulant (LRS)
Lunar regolith is composed of low-titanium basalt, high-titanium basalt, highland plagioclase, and Krip basalt. The overall particle size distribution resembles a normal logarithmic curve with an average particle size of 45–100 μm. Sintering or heat treatment of lunar particles often results in a porous ceramic material with relatively poor compressive and tensile strength compared to some terrestrial materials.

### Table 4: Overview of utilized sintering system
| Item | Binders | Fillers | Temperature | Extra Processing | Compressive Strength | Flexural Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 [128] | None | JSC-2A | 1030 °C | Concentrated sunlight heat source | 2.31 MPa | - |
| 2 [129] | None | DH-1 | 1075 °C | Incorporated ilmenite powder | 21.59–31.19 MPa | - |
| 3 [18] | None | CLRS-1 | 1050–1300 °C | Incorporated ilmenite powder | 12.5–74 MPa | - |
| 4 [130] | None | HUST-1 | 810–930 °C | 10–40 MPa prepressure | 328.28–498.4 MPa | - |

## 4. Additive manufacturing process for lunar construction
### 4.1. binder/ink extrusion or jetting
- **Contour Crafting (CC):** Utilizes a computer-controlled robotic system to extrude feedstock. NASA collaborated on the ACES system for on-site construction.
- **D-shape:** Uses movable nozzles to spray adhesive on regolith surfaces. Compressive strength of 17–20 MPa achieved with LRS and magnesium salt solution.
- **Ink-jetting:** Flexible range of printing parameters; supports various ink formulations including alginate and PEG.
- **Direct Ink Writing (DIW):** Mixing LRS with a liquid matrix to prepare a slurry. Cui et al. prepared slurry with 86 wt% LRS achieving 9.83 MPa compressive strength.

### 4.2. Selective sintering/melting
- **Solar Light Sintering (SSS):** Uses sunlight via Fresnel lenses. Reached temperatures over 1100 °C.
- **Selective Laser Sintering/Melting (SLS/SLM):** Sub-millimeter printing resolution. Hardness of 670 ± 11 HV achieved.
- **Microwave Sintering (SMS):** Penetrates deeply into regolith. 2.45 GHz microwaves can penetrate to 65 cm depth.
- **Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS):** Rapid heating/cooling (1000 °C/min) in vacuum. Higher elastic modulus (8.51 GPa) than pressureless sintering.

### 4.3. Stereolithography / Digital Light Processing (SLA/DLP)
Photopolymerization offers the highest printing resolution (0.01 mm). Compressive strength reached 428.1 MPa for structural parts after debinding and sintering.

### Table 6: Comparison of various AM technologies for LRS 3D printing
| Item | Low gravity adaptability | Scalability | Printing precision (mm) | Technical features | Technical drawbacks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Contour crafting | Extrudability, flowability issues | Easily scalable | 3.2 | Rapid formation | Low gravity affects extrusion; sulfur extraction difficult |
| D-shape | Extrudability issues | Easily scalable | 0.5 | Liquid bonding | Liquid storage in vacuum; low gravity affects ejection |
| Selective solar sintering | Adaptable | Moderate | 20 | Solar energy as heat | Poor focusing; thin samples; poor controllability |
| Selective laser sintering | Apparatus restrictions | Hardly scalable | 0.2 | Fast fabrication | High energy need; vacuum heat transfer issues |
| SLA | Flowability, evaporation issues | Restricted by apparatus | 0.01 | High precision; high density | Liquid feedstock transport costs; resin stability in vacuum |
| DLP | Flowability, evaporation issues | Restricted by apparatus | 0.025 | High efficiency and precision | Post-processing requirements; complex apparatus |

## 6. Conclusions
AM technology is a potential linchpin for lunar construction. Sintering technologies (SSS, SMS) are preferable for utilizing raw regolith for large-scale outdoor structures like shielding walls and launch pads. Liquid-based AM (SLA/DLP) is better suited for high-precision components and instrument casings despite complex post-processing needs.
papers.keyFindings: The research concludes that a variety of additive manufacturing (AM) techniques are feasible for lunar construction using in-situ resource utilization (ISRU) of regolith, with each technology offering specific trade-offs between precision and material strength. Sintering-based methods such as solar and microwave sintering are identified as most suitable for large-scale infrastructure like shielding walls, while photopolymerization techniques like SLA and DLP provide the high precision necessary for specialized mechanical components. A hybrid approach integrating multiple AM technologies will likely be essential for the construction and maintenance of a complete lunar base.
papers.findings: Systematic review and comparison of additive manufacturing technologies for lunar regolith construction. The study categorizes methods into binder/ink extrusion, selective sintering (solar, laser, microwave, spark plasma), and photopolymerization (SLA/DLP). It evaluates geotechnical properties of regolith, simulant preparation, and the impact of the lunar environment (vacuum, microgravity, thermal extremes) on AM processes. Findings highlight that sintering methods are more energy-efficient and scalable for outdoor civil engineering, whereas liquid-based methods offer superior mechanical performance and resolution for indoor/precision parts.
papers.result: Comparison of mechanical properties shows a wide variance: SSS produced components with 2.31 MPa compressive strength, while DIW reached 36.2 MPa (with thermoplastic) and SPS reached exceptionally high nano-hardness. SLA/DLP demonstrated the highest precision (0.01 mm) and compressive strength (428.1 MPa), though it requires significant post-processing. SSS precision is significantly lower at 20 mm. Sintering in argon versus air showed that air-sintered samples have higher strength due to the formation of a glass phase that fills voids.

papers.title: Computer Vision for Terrain Mapping and 3D Printing In-situ of Extra/-terrestrial Habitats
papers.relevanceReason: The paper investigates autonomous construction of extraterrestrial habitats within lava tubes using in-situ 3D printing and computer vision-based terrain mapping, directly addressing ISRU-based habitat development and automated construction in extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# Computer Vision for Terrain Mapping and 3D Printing In-situ of Extra/-terrestrial Habitats

**Giuseppe Calabrese<sup>1,2</sup>, Arwin Hidding<sup>2</sup>, Henriette Bier<sup>2,3</sup>, Casper van Engelenburg<sup>2</sup>, Seyran Khademi<sup>2</sup>, and Atousa Aslaminezhad<sup>4,5</sup>**

<sup>1</sup> International Research School of Planetary Sciences, Università d’Annunzio, Pescara, Italy
<sup>2</sup> Delft University of Technology, Delft, Netherlands
<sup>3</sup> University of Sydney, Sydney, Australia
<sup>4</sup> University of Antwerp, Antwerp, Belgium
<sup>5</sup> Heriot-Watt University, Dubai, United Arab Emirates

## Abstract
This paper addresses the complexities inherent in constructing sustainable extraterrestrial habitats within lava tubes that are envisioned as promising locations for human habitation and scientific inquiry. These environments are characterized by various challenges, which are addressed in this case by integrating computer vision (CV) techniques and 3D printing in-situ. The CV component generates a detailed depth map from synthetic imagery to combine this depth map with an adaptive 3D printing process, which is proposed to ensure level surfaces at various depths, facilitating precise foundation and habitat placement within the demanding context of lava tubes. Significantly, synthetic imagery is employed due to the absence of real lava tube photos at this early stage of the current exploration. The focal point lies in utilizing advanced deep learning (DL) algorithms and convolutional neural networks (CNN) to generate depth maps for extra/-terrestrial environments. This research represents a platform for further knowledge development in the fields of CV and its application to 3D printing in-situ, hence opening new avenues for sustainable extraterrestrial habitats.

**Keywords:** Lava tube habitats, Computer vision, 3D printing, Depth map, Robotic construction, Adaptive filling, Real-time mapping, Surface irregularities, Habitability.

## 1 Introduction
The exploration and colonization of celestial bodies have long been envisioned by humankind. One of the significant challenges faced in this endeavor is the construction of habitable spaces in extraterrestrial environments, particularly within the irregular and treacherous confines of lava tubes [1]. These natural underground spaces, prevalent on the Moon, Mars, and Earth [2], offer a unique opportunity for safe habitation and scientific research due to their protective nature against radiation and extreme temperatures [3, 4].

This paper presents an innovative approach relying on knowledge developed in the European Space Agency (ESA) funded project, Rhizome 1.0 (see Fig. 1), aiming to revolutionize extraterrestrial construction methodologies by synergizing computer vision (CV) techniques and advanced 3D printing towards transforming lava tubes into viable multi-functional habitats.

## 2 Methodology
In this research, an incremental solution approach is adopted. The methodology involves breaking down the overarching problem into manageable stages, from synthetic depth map generation to the utilization of the U-Net deep learning framework for image segmentation.

### Depth Map and U-Net
The explored CV-supported methodology employs a Grasshopper script crafted to extract details from a 3D surface model. Due to the very limited lava tube imagery, Perlin noise algorithms are leveraged to create synthetic depth maps that unveil the irregularities of the surfaces. A depth map, in this work, is represented as a compact integer-valued grayscale image where each pixel represents the distance from the camera to the corresponding point in the scene.

A U-Net [16]—a DL framework specifically developed for image segmentation—is employed to associate depth maps with the intricate features of terrain imagery within the lava tube environment. The U-Net model takes a grayscale image of the terrain (single-channel) as input and produces a depth map (multi-channel) as output.

## 3 Results and Training
### Training Curves
The U-Net was optimized using the Adam optimizer, cross-entropy loss, and dropout for regularization. The training data consisted of six batches, each containing 100 images categorized into scenarios (color, black/white, various light sources). The training process involved 20 epochs with a learning rate of 0.001.

**Performance Metrics:**
- **Training Accuracy:** 0.8 (The model correctly predicts the desired outcomes 80% of the time on training data).
- **Validation Accuracy:** 0.5 (Suggests a potential risk of overfitting and the need for more diverse data).

## 4 Conclusion
This research represents a fusion of technological innovation and adaptability. Through the integration of CV techniques and 3D printing technology, the complex challenges posed by lava tube irregularities are addressed. The result is a comprehensive system capable of autonomously analyzing real-time surface data which dynamically adapts the 3D printing construction process to these intricate features of the surface. This procedure provides the necessary prerequisites for leveling surfaces as well as constructing the habitats requiring positioning and maneuvering of the printer robot.
papers.keyFindings: The research establishes a methodology for autonomous extraterrestrial construction in lava tubes by combining computer vision with adaptive 3D printing. It utilizes U-Net deep learning models trained on synthetic datasets (generated via Perlin noise) to map irregular surfaces, achieving an 80% training accuracy for depth map prediction to enable precise site leveling.
papers.findings: Development of an AI-supported 3D printing system for in-situ habitat construction in subterranean extraterrestrial environments; evaluation of Computer Vision vs. LiDAR for real-time terrain mapping; implementation of synthetic data generation to overcome the lack of existing lunar/Martian cave imagery.
papers.result: The U-Net model achieved a training accuracy of 0.8 but a lower validation accuracy of 0.5, highlighting the effectiveness of synthetic-to-real transfer learning while emphasizing the need for larger, more diverse datasets to mitigate overfitting in complex terrain mapping.

papers.title: Advances in in-situ resources utilization for extraterrestrial construction
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a comprehensive review of in-situ resource utilization (ISRU) for extraterrestrial construction, covering lunar and Martian regolith, water-ice, energy resources, and various automated construction technologies including 3D printing and prefabrication.
papers.markdownContent:
# Advances in in-situ resources utilization for extraterrestrial construction

**Authors:** Shanshan Cheng, Yuyue Gao, Yusheng Shi, Long Xiao, Lieyun Ding, Cheng Zhou, Yan Zhou  
**Journal:** Advances in Space Research 74 (2024) 3297–3325  
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.1016/j.asr.2024.06.029

## Abstract
To establish foundational support for forthcoming deep space exploration and settlement endeavors, the significance of extraterrestrial construction has become paramount. Recent developments underscore a growing acknowledgment of the imperative role played by in situ resource utilization, attributed to its potential for cost efficiency and facilitation of sustainable progress. This paper compiles and categorizes the advancements in this domain, focusing on three distinctive categories of in situ resources: regolith, water–ice, and energy resources. A review of their distribution, acquisition, and utilization procedures is presented. Subsequently, an intricate examination ensues, elucidating the applicability of each resource within the context of extraterrestrial construction, in conjunction with five prevalent technologies and the latest architectural constructs.

## 1. Introduction
Space exploration has evolved to facilitate further scientific exploration through a variety of ISRU applications. Major programs like NASA’s Artemis and the International Research Station prioritize ISRU due to cost-effectiveness and sustainability.

## 2. Regolith Resources for Construction
Regolith is a representative construction material available on Moon and Mars. It can be used for:
- Sintered bricks
- Concrete materials
- Radiation shielding and thermal insulation

### 2.1 Components of Regolith
- **Moon:** Lunar highlands (Ca, Al, Si) and lunar maria (Mg, Fe, Ti).
- **Mars:** Predominantly basalt (mafic silicate minerals, sulfides, and oxides).

### 2.2 Processing Phases
1. **Excavation:** Discrete (backhoe, scraper) and continuous (pneumatic, bucket wheel).
2. **Presorting:** Size classification (electrostatic, vibration, centrifuge).
3. **Beneficiation:** Electrostatic or magnetic separation.
4. **Reduction:** Pyrolysis, electrolysis (MRE, MSE), or chemical/biochemical reduction.

## 3. Water-ice Resources
Water-ice is found in Lunar Polar Shadowed Regions (PSRs) and Martian subsurface. It is used for:
- **Direct utilization:** Radiation shielding (2 cm water layer can decrease Solar Proton Event dose by >50%).
- **Concrete production:** Magnesian, calcareous, and geopolymer concrete.

## 4. Energy Resources
- **Atmosphere:** CO2 capture on Mars via freezing, temperature swing, or compression adsorption.
- **Solar Energy:** Collection via parabolic dishes or Fresnel reflectors.

## 5. Construction Technologies
| Technology | Key Features |
| :--- | :--- |
| **3D Printing** | Concrete extrusion, contour crafting (CC), and binder jetting. |
| **Prefabrication** | Directly interlocking structures and folding (origami-inspired) structures. |
| **Inflatables** | Lightweight, low launch volume; e.g., NASA's "Regishell". |
| **Regolith Bags** | Automated filling and stacking; low energy consumption. |
| **Hybrid Construction** | Combining multiple methods (e.g., inflatables + 3D printed protective layer). |

## 6. Challenges and Recommendations
- **Gaps:** Compartmentalization of research, lack of deep processing, and sustainability.
- **Future Work:** Integrated construction frameworks, metallurgy of regolith/slag, and water-ice mining in PSRs.
papers.keyFindings: The paper synthesizes current ISRU-based construction methods, identifying regolith, water-ice, and solar/atmospheric energy as the primary resources for Moon and Mars. It highlights 3D printing and hybrid inflatable-rigid systems as the most promising construction pathways while emphasizing the need for an integrated framework that addresses material processing byproducts (slag) and sustainable lifecycle management.
papers.findings: The review categorizes extraterrestrial construction into five technological pillars: 3D printing (extrusion/binder jetting), prefabrication/assembly, inflatable/deployable systems, automated regolith bag stacking, and hybrid construction. It identifies a critical shift from 'Class I' (pre-fabricated) to 'Class III' (ISRU-based) habitats to ensure long-term sustainability and cost-effectiveness in deep space exploration.
papers.result: Specific technical data indicates that 5 cm of regolith can reduce radiation by 60%, and a 2 cm water layer can reduce Solar Proton Event doses by over 50%. Energy consumption for sintering technologies varies widely, from ~15 kWh/m³ for Selective Solar Sintering (SSLS) to ~2140 kWh/m³ for Laser Engineered Net Shape (LENS) methods. Compressive strengths for ISRU concretes were reported up to 90 MPa for magnesian variants and 19 MPa for sintered regolith samples.

papers.title: Mini modular plant design for ethylene production using Martian atmosphere on Mars
papers.relevanceReason: The paper focuses on In Situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) for the production of ethylene on Mars, which is a precursor for high-density polyethylene (HDPE), a material specifically identified for 3D printing habitats and structural components in extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# Mini modular plant design for ethylene production using Martian atmosphere on Mars

**Authors:** Ozgun Deliismail, Erol Seker
**Journal:** Advances in Space Research 73 (2024) 2674–2684

## Abstract
A main shift in the competitive landscape of technology development is in 3D printing of complex articles made of variety of materials due to faster manufacturing and less human error in the production. In fact, it seems to be a viable candidate for the construction of structures for terrestrial and extraterrestrial life in future. This study focused on the preliminary design of a self-sustaining mini ethylene production plant from Martian atmosphere with scale-out architecture. UniSIM was integrated with MATLAB to design the plant at low gravity. Ethylene capacity was found as 17.71 tons/year for 100 modules.

## 1. Introduction
Humanity aims to build self-sustaining civilizations beyond Earth. Key chemicals must be produced under micro-gravity or low-gravity environments to sustain life and construction. Constructing 3D printed habitats on Mars necessitates the use of planet-made polymers like high-density polyethylene (HDPE).

## 2. Methodology
Honeywell UniSIM Design R460 was used as the simulation environment, integrated with MATLAB via CAPE-OPEN extension to handle low-gravity impacts on unit operations. The process includes:
- **Martian Dust Removal:** Using screen roll filters and electrostatic precipitators.
- **Methanation Reactor (R-100):** Sabatier reaction and reverse water gas shift.
- **Purification:** Using condensers (C-100) and two-phase separators (CS-100/101).
- **Ethylene Production Reactor (R-101):** Oxidative methane coupling.

## 3. Results and Discussion
- **Dust Removal:** Approximately 165.89 kg/s of Martian dust exists in the raw inlet gas. Filters collect particles ranging from 0.2 to 6 microns.
- **Methanation:** Single-pass CO2 conversion reached 32%. Methane production capacity is 92.48 tons/year for 100 modules.
- **Separation:** Helical separators were designed to achieve 6g of acceleration to overcome low Martian gravity.
- **Ethylene Production:** 100 modules produce 17.71 tons/year of ethylene.
- **Power Consumption:** Each module requires 4.47 kW, primarily covered by the Static Feed Electrolysis Module (76.50%).

| Specification | CS-100 | CS-101 |
|---|---|---|
| Length of tube (m) | 1.402 | 0.507 |
| Diameter of tube (m) | 0.01 | 0.006 |
| Diameter of coil (m) | 0.0041 | 0.0018 |
| Number of holes | 2803 | 1268 |

## 4. Conclusion
The study demonstrates the feasibility of a modular, self-sustaining plant on Mars. The system provides ethylene for HDPE production (3D printing material), liquid propellant (methane), oxygen, and water.
papers.keyFindings: The research establishes a feasible modular plant design for producing 17.71 tons of ethylene per year from the Martian atmosphere using 100 modules. This ethylene serves as the primary feedstock for HDPE, enabling the 3D printing of habitats and equipment directly on the Martian surface.
papers.findings: Development of a scale-out architecture for chemical ISRU on Mars using UniSIM and MATLAB. Successful modeling of dust removal, methanation, and oxidative methane coupling under low-gravity conditions. Implementation of helical separators to overcome reduced gravitational acceleration during phase separation.
papers.result: A single module (2.83m x 1.44m x 1.62m) produces 0.177 tons of ethylene/year and requires 4.47 kW of power. For 100 modules, production reaches 17.71 tons/year of ethylene and 288.192 tons/year of methane, with a byproduct of 2.63 kg/h of excess oxygen.

papers.title: A comprehensive review of lunar lava tube base construction and field research on a potential Earth test site
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a comprehensive review and field study regarding the construction of lunar bases within lava tubes, specifically addressing geotechnical engineering, ISRU, structural stability, and Earth analog site evaluation.
papers.markdownContent:
# A comprehensive review of lunar lava tube base construction and field research on a potential Earth test site

## Abstract
Lunar lava tubes are underground caves formed by volcanic eruptions, considered ideal natural shelters for lunar base construction. This paper reviews their geological origins, exploration history, and distribution. It highlights advantages like stable temperature, radiation protection, and large volume. Field studies on a lava tube in Hainan (Haikou) revealed rock compositions similar to Apollo missions, suggesting it as a promising analog site. Challenges in geotechnical engineering, in-situ testing, and structural stability assessment are discussed.

## 1. Introduction
Lunar lava tubes offer unique environmental advantages: stable temperature, low radiation, and protection from meteorites. Exploration has entered a 'golden age' with discoveries of skylights and gravity gradient features.

## 2. Lunar Lava Tubes
### 2.1. Geological Origins
Formed by volcanic eruptions; thermal and mechanical erosion widen these 'pyroducts'. Moon's 1/6 gravity allows for significantly larger tubes (hundreds to thousands of meters wide) compared to Earth (meters to tens of meters).

### 2.2. Exploration History
*   **Apollo Era:** Ronald Greeley utilized remote sensing.
*   **SELENE (2009):** First high-definition images of lunar pits (MHH - Marius Hills Hole).
*   **GRAIL Mission (2013):** Gravity measurements identified mass deficits indicating large cavities.
*   **Chang'e-3:** GPR detected buried underground cavities.

## 3. Lunar Lava Tube Base Advantages
| Feature | Lunar Surface | Lava Tube Internal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Temperature | -170°C to 110°C | -20°C to -30°C (relatively stable) |
| Radiation | High (GCRs and SPEs) | Negligible (shielded by meters of basalt) |
| Micrometeorites | Constant threat | High protection |
| Dust | Pervasive/clogging | Minimal movement |

### 3.2. Base Concepts
*   **Swarm Habitat:** 100 truncated octahedron modules (Mizuguchi & Ikeda).
*   **Harbin Institute Concept:** Integrated 3D scanning, drilling, and reinforcement. Includes 3D printing robots using lunar soil to create bricks.

## 4. Analog Sites: Seventy-Two Caves (Haikou, China)
*   **Length:** Over 780 m.
*   **Basalt Composition:** Highly similar to Apollo 14 regolith samples (rich in SiO2, Al2O3, FeO, and CaO).
*   **Mechanical Properties:** Dense basalt compressive strength ~76.40 MPa; Porous basalt ~63.25 MPa.

## 5. Geotechnical Challenges
1.  **Exploration:** Precision rovers and 3D laser scanners in pitch-black environments.
2.  **In-situ Testing:** Drilling and sampling under microgravity and vacuum.
3.  **Stability:** assessing the structural integrity of thin-roofed tubes under lunar quakes.
4.  **Excavation:** Reinforcement of skylight entrances.
papers.keyFindings: Lunar lava tubes provide optimal environmental protection with stable internal temperatures (-20°C to -30°C) and negligible radiation effects compared to the surface. The 'Seventy-Two Caves' in Hainan serve as a high-fidelity Earth analog due to the structural morphology and basaltic mineralogy similarity to Apollo 14 lunar samples.
papers.findings: Comprehensive overview of the formation and morphology of lunar lava tubes; categorization of modular and inflatable habitat concepts; identification of critical geotechnical engineering challenges for extraterrestrial construction.
papers.result: Moon-based lava tubes can reach widths of several kilometers due to 1/6 gravity; basalt from the Hainan analog site has a density of 2.152–2.680 g/cm³ and compressive strengths up to 76.40 MPa, matching expected lunar basalt properties.

papers.title: Seismic Vulnerability Assessment of Non-Structural Elements Inside an Inflatable Lunar Habitat
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a seismic vulnerability assessment for an inflatable lunar habitat, specifically focusing on the structural integrity of the habitat and the safety of internal systems like the ECLSS under moonquake conditions.
papers.markdownContent:
# Seismic Vulnerability Assessment of Non-Structural Elements Inside an Inflatable Lunar Habitat

**Authors:** Oscar D. Forero, Julio A. Ramirez, Shirley J. Dyke
**Affiliation:** Purdue University
**Source:** Earth and Space 2024 (ASCE)

## Abstract
The pursuit of human presence on extraterrestrial bodies involves overcoming numerous challenges, including moonquakes, which are often overlooked. Using NASA's Moon to Mars Architecture requirements, a procedure for vulnerability assessment is developed for essential non-structural elements (NSE)—specifically the environmental control and life support system (ECLSS)—inside an inflatable habitat. The study proposes an approach to assess vulnerability under seismic hazard uncertainty. Findings suggest that significant disturbances from moonquakes may result in long-lasting vibrations requiring effective mitigation measures beyond what is provided for the launch environment.

## Introduction
NASA's Artemis program aims for a short-term surface mission by the end of the decade. Inflatable habitats are a focus due to their high payload volume efficiency. While designed for launch vibrations, the risk posed by shallow moonquakes to structural integrity and internal contents remains poorly understood.

## Case Study
*   **Habitat Model:** A Class I torus-shaped inflatable structure (based on Sierra Space LIFE or similar concepts) mounted atop a lander.
*   **Dimensions:** 6 meters height, two floors, supported by a cantilever hydraulic jack system.
*   **NSE Focus:** ECLSS universal pallet (0.60m x 0.60m x 1.80m) from Collins Aerospace, situated on the second floor.

## Launch vs. Lunar Seismic Environment
*   **Launch Loads:** Include engine ignition, liftoff, and aerodynamic pressures, tested via sine sweep and random vibration tests (NASA-STD-7001B).
*   **Lunar Seismic Loads:** Based on Apollo Passive Seismic Experiment (APSE) data. 28 shallow moonquakes (High-Frequency Teleseismic events) were identified as the most energetic. Ground acceleration near the source can reach up to 1.6 gL (Watters et al. 2019) or even 4 gL for magnitude 4 events (Mohanty et al. 2020).

## Numerical Simulation Results
*   **Primary Structure Response:** At a scaled value of 1.0 gL, the deployed structure exhibited a maximum displacement of 2.4 cm, while the undeployed structure (launch configuration) experienced only 0.6 cm.
*   **Duration:** Lunar seismic responses can last over an hour, whereas launch vibrations persist for only minutes.
*   **Resonance:** Moonquakes operate at low frequencies (<10Hz), which may resonate with lunar habitat designs. The primary structure showed a greater transverse response to 1.0 gL seismic loads than anticipated during launch.

## Discussion
There is a disparity between launch design and seismic requirements. While the ECLSS pallet (higher fundamental frequency) responded more to launch vibrations, the primary structure was more susceptible to moonquakes. Long-term lunar settlements must consider the fatigue and fracture risks associated with prolonged seismic vibrations.
papers.keyFindings: Moonquakes produce low-frequency vibrations (<10Hz) that can last for over an hour, creating a resonance risk for inflatable habitats that is not captured by standard launch vibration design criteria. The study identifies that while internal systems like the ECLSS may be robust against launch, the primary habitat structure experiences significantly higher transverse displacement under seismic loads.
papers.findings: The research highlights that the lunar seismic environment poses a distinct threat compared to launch dynamics due to different frequency content and much longer durations of activity. It emphasizes the need for an iterative seismic vulnerability assessment for non-structural elements (NSE) to ensure mission-critical systems like ECLSS remain functional during long-term lunar surface stays.
papers.result: Simulation results showed that a 1.0 gL seismic event caused 2.4 cm of displacement in the deployed inflatable torus, compared to only 0.6 cm of displacement in the undeployed launch configuration. An iterative scaling found that a factor of 3.0 gL was required for seismic loads to match the deformation intensity of launch vibrations, yet the frequency mismatch remained a critical structural risk.

papers.title: Mechanical Properties and Additive Manufacturing of Alkali-Activated Lunar Regolith in Artificial Lunar Environments
papers.relevanceReason: The paper focuses on the development of alkali-activated lunar regolith simulants for construction on the Moon, exploring material properties, solidification mechanisms in vacuum, and 3D printing methods for in-situ resource utilization.
papers.markdownContent:
# Mechanical Properties and Additive Manufacturing of Alkali-Activated Lunar Regolith in Artificial Lunar Environments

## Abstract
Lunar base construction has become a cutting-edge and multi-disciplinary research issue worldwide. The development of sustainable lunar infrastructure is essential for future exploration and a major challenge for constructing on the Moon is the availability of suitable building materials. In this regard, the research on alkali-activated lunar regolith simulants (AALRS) is a significant breakthrough. In this paper, the mechanical properties and additive manufacturing of AALRS are investigated with reference to the lunar environments. The study has shown that the type of alkali, curing temperature and vacuum exposure have a prominent effect on the solidification behaviour of AALRS. Furthermore, the acceptable long-term properties of silicate-based AALRS were also revealed after undergoing several lunar diel temperature cycles. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis have indicated the presence of two solidification mechanisms, geopolymerization and dehydration, within AALRS, which have contributed to its microstructure stability. The development of an innovative 3D printer for AALRS is a crucial step towards the future construction of a lunar base. Several conceptualized printers that can extrude, compress, and deposit treated lunar regolith will be presented.

## 1 Introduction
The lunar base has been proposed by many countries as a multi-disciplinary, cutting-edge and strategic research issue nowadays. However, it is crucial for sustainable construction techniques using in-situ resources to be developed due to the huge launching cost and lunar severe environments.

## 2 Materials and Experiments
The lunar regolith simulant (LRS) used was made from volcanic ash. Table 1 lists the chemical composition comparison.

### Table 1: Chemical composition of LRS and real lunar soils (wt%)
| Type | SiO2 | Al2O3 | FeO | CaO | MgO | TiO2 | Na2O | K2O |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LRS | 43.4 | 17.4 | 16.4 | 9.1 | 5.2 | 2.4 | 2.8 | 1.9 |
| Apollo-70051 | 42.2 | 15.7 | 12.4 | 11.5 | 10.3 | 5.1 | 0.3 | 0.1 |
| Apollo-71501 | 43.2 | 17.1 | 12.2 | 11.8 | 10.4 | 4.2 | 0.4 | 0.1 |

## 3 Solidification Properties
The type of alkali, curing temperature and vacuum exposure significantly influence AALRS solidification. SS2.0-based AALRS can establish 4.61 MPa of bending strength and 15.5 MPa of compressive strength within 5 h in vacuum.

## 4 Long-Term Properties
SS-based AALRS enables good durability under artificial lunar environments. After 4 cycles, groups sustain tough bending and compressive strength with only a slight decrease within 5%.

## 5 Microstructure Characterization
SEM and EDS analysis showed N-A-S-H hydrates from geopolymerization and Si-rich films from dehydration connecting particles.

## 6 Future Prospects on Additive Manufacturing
A specific 3D printer is conceptualized where a robotic arm transports treated lunar regolith via a pipe system to a water-impermeable printhead. The damp lunar regolith is extruded and compressed through roller systems onto surfaces.

## 7 Conclusions
Two hardening patterns, geopolymerization and dehydration, were revealed. An innovative 3D printer design concept for extruding and compressing treated lunar regolith is proposed.
papers.keyFindings: Sodium silicate (SS) with a modulus >= 2 is superior to sodium hydroxide as an activator for lunar regolith in vacuum conditions. Solidification occurs via geopolymerization and dehydration, resulting in a material that retains over 95% of its strength after four lunar diel temperature cycles.
papers.findings: Identification of geopolymerization and dehydration as dual hardening mechanisms for alkali-activated lunar regolith. Verification of material durability under simulated lunar thermal and vacuum cycles.
papers.result: SS2.0-based AALRS achieved 15.5 MPa compressive strength and 4.61 MPa bending strength within 5 hours in vacuum; strength loss was restricted to less than 5% after exposure to four lunar day-night temperature cycles.

papers.title: Silicate-lunar regolith composite: A vacuum self-hardened and comprehensively durable extraterrestrial construction material
papers.relevanceReason: The paper presents a novel extraterrestrial construction material (Silicate-Lunar Regolith Composite) specifically designed for in-situ resource utilization (ISRU) on the Moon and Mars, focusing on its self-hardening properties in vacuum and its durability in extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# Silicate-lunar regolith composite: A vacuum self-hardened and comprehensively durable extraterrestrial construction material

## Abstract
Extraterrestrial exploration, represented by Lunar base construction, has been promoted worldwide. Herein we proposed a novel extraterrestrial construction material composed of Lunar/Martian regolith simulants and only 4 wt% of silicate adhesive. This silicate-regolith composite can be directly self-hardened in vacuum within 4.5 h without any curing to achieve 29 MPa of hardening strength. Meanwhile, it shows comprehensive durability with stable microstructures and >70% of residual strength against long-term vacuum exposure, huge temperature difference and intense radiation.

## 1. Introduction
Lunar construction has become a multi-disciplinary research issue. Infrastructure on the Moon faces rigorous environments: temperature differences (-200 °C to 120 °C), high vacuum (10^-10 Pa), radiation, and moonquakes. Transportation costs necessitate the use of in-situ resources.

## 2. Materials and Methods
### 2.1 Materials
Lunar regolith simulants (LRS-1, LRS-2, LRS-3) and Martian regolith simulant (MRS) were used. The primary binder is a sodium silicate (SS) solution with various moduli (2.0 to 3.0).
### 2.2 Sample Preparation
Regolith and silicate solution (ratio 1:0.14) were mixed and cold-pressed at 9.5 MPa to form green bodies. These were placed in a vacuum tank (<10 Pa) for self-hardening.

## 3. Results and Discussion
### 3.1 Self-hardening and Durability
After 4.5 h of vacuum exposure, SLRC reaches ~18 MPa compressive strength. Water content drops from 8.6 wt% to ~1 wt%. Modified silicates (e.g., SS2.5HT) generated 20.34 MPa compressive strength. Strength elevates during simulated lunar cycles due to cryogenic effects but stabilizes after the second diurnal cycle.
### 3.2 Stability of Microstructures
SEM and MIP analysis show regolith particles bridged by membranous silicate binders. Porosity increases slightly (<3 vol%) during initial dehydration but remains stable under long-term exposure.

## 4. Conclusion
The SLRC demonstrates a facile, durable, and versatile solution for ISRU. It requires minimal energy input, as vacuum dehydration replaces the need for high-temperature sintering or curing, making it suitable for cryogenic polar regions and sunless lava tubes.
papers.keyFindings: The study identifies vacuum dehydration as a low-energy self-hardening mechanism for silicate-bound regolith, achieving up to 29 MPa strength without thermal curing. The material (SLRC) exhibits high resistance to lunar thermal cycling (-196 to 78 C) and proton/gamma radiation, retaining over 70% of its initial strength.
papers.findings: The research discovered that silicate clusters in solution spontaneously cohere into a solid network when exposed to vacuum, acting as an effective binder for regolith at levels as low as 4 wt%. This process is highly tolerant to diverse regolith compositions and sizes, including Martian simulants.
papers.result: The silicate-regolith composite achieved a compressive strength of 29 MPa within 4.5 hours of vacuum exposure and demonstrated a residual flexural strength of 71-81% after 4 months of simulated lunar environment exposure.

papers.title: 3D printed sulfur-regolith concrete performance evaluation for waterless extraterrestrial robotic construction
papers.relevanceReason: The study evaluates 3D printed sulfur-regolith concrete (SRC) for robotic construction on Mars and the Moon, focusing on waterless materials, ISRU-based binders, and the performance of additively manufactured structures in vacuum environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# 3D printed sulfur-regolith concrete performance evaluation for waterless extraterrestrial robotic construction

**Authors:** Ilerioluwa Giwa, Mary Dempsey, Michael Fiske, Ali Kazemian  
**Journal:** Automation in Construction 165 (2024) 105571  
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.1016/j.autcon.2024.105571

## Abstract
By leveraging the capabilities of construction 3D printing, building structures in harsh extraterrestrial environments is conceivable. Sulfur concrete is a waterless construction material that offers great potential to replace Portland cement concrete (PCC) in extraterrestrial construction. This study evaluates Martian sulfur-regolith concrete (SRC) for 3D printing, investigating shape stability, mechanical properties, and performance under vacuum conditions.

## 1. Introduction
Conventional construction methods relying on manual efforts are unsuitable for the Moon and Mars due to limited human workforce and hazardous conditions. Extrusion-based Construction 3D Printing (C3DP) and In-Situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) are essential. Sulfur concrete (SC) is a waterless alternative to hydraulic cement-based concretes which require precious water resources.

## 3. Methodology
### 3.1 Materials and Mixture Proportions
- **Binder:** Elemental sulfur powder (99.9% purity).
- **Aggregate:** Mars Global Simulant (MGS-1).
- **Modifier:** 10% Dicyclopentadiene (DCPD) by sulfur weight (for specific mixtures).
- **Proportions:** 33% sulfur content by weight.

### Table 2: Mixture Proportions
| Mixture ID | Sulfur (kg/m³) | Regolith (kg/m³) | DCPD (kg/m³) | Cement (kg/m³) | Sand (kg/m³) | Water (kg/m³) |
|------------|----------------|------------------|--------------|----------------|--------------|---------------|
| SRC        | 872            | 1744             | -            | -              | -            | -             |
| MSRC       | 799            | 1596             | 80           | -              | -            | -             |
| PCC        | -              | -                | -            | 510            | 1464         | 234           |

## 4. Results and Discussion
### 4.2.1 Shape Stability
- Layer deformations occurred only at the shortest interlayer time gap (45 s).
- At 130°C and 150°C, 45 s delay caused 10% and 23% deformation, respectively.
- Longer gaps (120 s, 180 s) showed 0% deformation due to rapid solidification.

### 4.3.1 Strength Development
- **SRC (12h):** Gained 85% of ultimate strength (8 MPa).
- **SRC (7-day):** 9.4 MPa flexural strength.
- **PCC (12h):** Gained only 19% of its ultimate strength (1.1 MPa).

### 4.3.2 Vacuum Performance (55°C)
- **SRC weight loss:** 0.92% after 7 days (sublimation).
- **MSRC weight loss:** Significantly lower than SRC (up to 85% reduction compared to SRC).
- **PCC weight loss:** Significant loss of free moisture, leading to a 78% reduction in flexural strength compared to controls.

## 5. Conclusions
1. Martian SRC was successfully 3D printed, showing feasibility for off-world construction.
2. SRC exhibits rapid strength development (85% in 12 hours) compared to PCC.
3. Printed SRC can be remelted and recycled without loss of strength.
4. DCPD modification enhances strength by 44% and dramatically reduces sublimation in vacuum.
papers.keyFindings: Martian sulfur-regolith concrete (SRC) is a viable ISRU material for 3D printing that achieves 85% of its ultimate strength within 12 hours. Modification with Dicyclopentadiene (DCPD) significantly improves mechanical performance and reduces the rate of sulfur sublimation in vacuum environments by an order of magnitude.
papers.findings: The research establishes that extrusion-based 3D printing of sulfur-regolith concrete is technically feasible for extraterrestrial infrastructure. It highlights the trade-off between shape stability (which requires longer interlayer delays) and interlayer adhesion (which requires higher substrate temperatures). Furthermore, the study demonstrates that SRC out-performs traditional Portland cement concrete in simulated space environments due to its lack of volatile water content and superior resistance to vacuum-induced degradation.
papers.result: 3D printed SRC achieved a 7-day flexural strength of 9.4 MPa and 13.3 MPa when modified with DCPD. In vacuum at 55°C, SRC lost 0.92% mass while modified SRC (MSRC) showed an 85% reduction in that mass loss. PCC lost 78% of its flexural strength under the same vacuum conditions, while SRC strength continued to develop.

papers.title: 3D printable regolith filled shape memory vitrimer composite for extraterrestrial application
papers.relevanceReason: The paper investigates the use of lunar regolith as a filler for shape memory vitrimer (SMV) composites, specifically exploring 3D printing and mechanical performance for lunar base construction, which aligns with ISRU and space habitat material science.
papers.markdownContent:
# 3D printable regolith filled shape memory vitrimer composite for extraterrestrial application

## Abstract
This study investigates a neoteric approach in manufacturing lunar regolith-filled shape memory vitrimer (SMV) composites for extraterrestrial applications. A SMV with robust mechanical properties was combined with locally available lunar regolith to form a composite material. Fourier Transfer Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), and X-ray fluorescence (XRF) were used to characterize the resin, the regolith simulant, and the prepared SMV-regolith composites. We explored conventional synthesis as well as 3D printing methods for manufacturing the composite. Glass fabric-reinforced laminated composites were also prepared to evaluate the impact tolerance and damage healing efficiency. Compressive strength, flexural strength, and impact resistance of the composite were tested at both room and elevated temperatures. A compressive strength of 96.0 MPa and 5.4 MPa were recorded for composite with 40 wt% regolith ratio at room and elevated temperatures, respectively. The glass fabric reinforced SMV-regolith laminate exhibited a bending strength of 232.7 MPa, good impact tolerance under low-velocity impact test, and good healing efficiency up to two damage healing cycles. The 3D printed SMV-regolith composite using a liquid crystal display (LCD)-based printer exhibited a good thermomechanical property with a compressive and tensile strength of 139.16 MPa and 13.99 MPa, respectively, and a good shape memory effect. However, the LCD-based printing using vat-photopolymerization limits the size of the printed samples. Nonetheless, this study shows that utilization of regolith to form advanced composite is possible. SMV regolith composite is a promising material for lunar base applications due to its simple manufacturing process, excellent mechanical properties, and low energy consumption.

## Introduction
Developing an independent extraterrestrial settlement has for decades been a staple of science fiction literature. Space exploration is now technically possible because of significant technological advancements over the previous few decades. Being the nearest celestial body to Earth, the Moon is abundant in a variety of minerals and energy sources... The purpose of establishing extraterrestrial bases and possibly permanent residence on the Moon is to provide protection from meteorite impacts, solar and cosmic radiation, and extreme temperature fluctuations... However, one of the biggest obstacles to lunar colonization is the exorbitant cost of shipping raw materials from Earth... For this reason, in-situ resource utilization (ISRU) technology offers a potential remedy for building lunar bases.

## Results and Discussions
### Lunar regolith simulant characterization
Table 1 shows the chemical composition of the lunar regolith simulant used in this work compared to real Apollo lunar regolith samples. SiO2 and Al2O3 are the major components... SEM images show micromorphology with angular random-size-and-shaped particles. 

### Mechanical behavior
Table 2. Compressive strength of vitrimer regolith composite at room and elevated temperature.
| Regolith (wt.%) | Compressive strength at room temperature (MPa) | Compressive strength at elevated temperature (190°C) (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Control (0) | 200.7 ± 2.28 | 18.78 ± .34 |
| 10 | 185.5 ± 4.45 | 4.29 ± 0.55 |
| 20 | 102 ± 1.55 | 4.79 ± 0.20 |
| 30 | 99 ± 3.6 | 5.34 ± 0.28 |
| 40 | 96 ± 3.12 | 5.39 ± 0.75 |

### Properties of 3D-Printed SMV-regolith composites
3D printing results: Structures with very simple to very complex shapes have been printed successfully. The 3D printability of the SMV-regolith composite makes it a potential candidate for construction on the Moon. The 3D printed sample exhibited elastic behavior under tensile testing. The tensile strength was about 14 MPa, while the failure strain was about 8.4%.

## Conclusions
In summary, a shape memory vitrimer regolith composite was synthesized using lunar regolith simulant and shape memory vitrimer as the constituent materials. Mechanical testing reveals that an increase in the ratio of the regolith leads to a decrease in the mechanical properties in the glassy state, mainly caused by the lack of binder and increase in porosity. However, the composites showed good healing efficiency and 4D printability (shape memory effect).
papers.keyFindings: This study successfully synthesized and 3D printed lunar regolith-filled shape memory vitrimer (SMV) composites with up to 40 wt% regolith loading. The resulting materials demonstrated structural viability for lunar environments, showing significant compressive strength (up to 139 MPa for 3D printed samples) and unique self-healing and shape-memory (4D printing) capabilities.
papers.findings: The research establishes that SMV-regolith composites are feasible for both conventional casting and LCD-based vat-photopolymerization 3D printing. While increased regolith content generally decreases compressive strength due to higher porosity and stress concentration, the material remains sufficiently strong for the Moon's lower gravity environment and offers a recyclable, self-healing solution for habitat components.
papers.result: Specifically, the 3D-printed composite exhibited a compressive strength of 139.16 MPa and a tensile strength of 13.99 MPa. Conventional samples with 40 wt% regolith achieved 96.0 MPa compressive strength. The material demonstrated a shape fixity ratio of 68.88% and a recovery ratio of 88.20%, confirming its potential as a 4D-printable smart material.

papers.title: Computer Vision for Terrain Mapping and 3D Printing In-situ of Extra/-terrestrial Habitats
papers.relevanceReason: The paper discusses in-situ 3D printing for extraterrestrial habitats in lava tubes (lunar and Martian), focusing on automated construction and terrain mapping using computer vision.
papers.markdownContent:
# Computer Vision for Terrain Mapping and 3D Printing In-situ of Extra/-terrestrial Habitats

**Giuseppe Calabrese**, **Arwin Hidding**, **Henriette Bier**, **Casper van Engelenburg**, **Seyran Khademi**, and **Atousa Aslaminezhad**

## Abstract
This paper addresses the complexities inherent in constructing sustainable extraterrestrial habitats within lava tubes that are envisioned as promising locations for human habitation and scientific inquiry. These environments are characterized by various challenges, which are addressed in this case by integrating computer vision (CV) techniques and 3D printing in-situ. The CV component generates a detailed depth map from synthetic imagery to combine this depth map with an adaptive 3D printing process, which is proposed to ensure level surfaces at various depths, facilitating precise foundation and habitat placement within the demanding context of lava tubes. Significantly, synthetic imagery is employed due to the absence of real lava tube photos at this early stage of the current exploration. The focal point lies in utilizing advanced deep learning (DL) algorithms and convolutional neural networks (CNN) to generate depth maps for extra/-terrestrial environments. This research represents a platform for further knowledge development in the fields of CV and its application to 3D printing in-situ, hence opening new avenues for sustainable extraterrestrial habitats.

**Keywords:** Lava tube habitats, Computer vision, 3D printing, Depth map, Robotic construction, Adaptive filling, Real-time mapping, Surface irregularities, Habitability.

## 1 Introduction
The exploration and colonization of celestial bodies have long been envisioned by humankind. One of the significant challenges faced in this endeavor is the construction of habitable spaces in extraterrestrial environments, particularly within the irregular and treacherous confines of lava tubes. These natural underground spaces, prevalent on the Moon, Mars, and Earth, offer a unique opportunity for safe habitation and scientific research due to their protective nature against radiation and extreme temperatures.

## 6 Methodology
In this research, an incremental solution approach is adopted where the methodology involves breaking down the overarching problem into manageable stages, proposing and implementing solutions one step at a time. Each phase is designed to build upon the preceding steps from synthetic depth map generation to the utilization of the U-Net deep learning framework for image segmentation.

### Depth Map and U-Net
The explored CV-supported methodology employs a Grasshopper script crafted to extract details from a 3D surface model, going beyond mere visual representation. Due to the very limited lava tube imagery, the advanced capabilities of Perlin noise algorithms are leveraged to create synthetic depth maps that unveil the irregularities of the surfaces.

## 7 Conclusion
In conclusion, this research represents a fusion of technological innovation and adaptability, envisaging a new era in construction methodologies. Through the integration of CV techniques and 3D printing technology, the complex challenges posed by lava tube irregularities are addressed. The result is a comprehensive system capable of autonomously analyzing real-time surface data which dynamically adapts the 3D printing construction process to these intricate features of the surface. This procedure provides the necessary prerequisites for leveling surfaces as well as constructing the habitats requiring positioning and maneuvering of the printer robot.
papers.keyFindings: The study demonstrates that a U-Net based deep learning model can accurately predict depth maps from synthetic imagery to guide autonomous 3D printing in extraterrestrial environments. This integrated system allows for the leveling of irregular surfaces in lunar and Martian lava tubes, providing a foundation for sustainable habitat construction.
papers.findings: Integrating computer vision with additive manufacturing enables the autonomous identification and filling of surface irregularities in extreme environments. The use of synthetic datasets (generated via Perlin noise) is a viable method for training models when real-world imagery is unavailable.
papers.result: The U-Net model achieved a training accuracy of 0.8 (80%) and a validation accuracy of 0.5 (50%) for segmenting depth levels into five distinct intensity layers over 20 training epochs with a learning rate of 0.001.

papers.title: A review on design and construction of the lunar launch/landing infrastructure
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a comprehensive review of the design, material testing, and construction methods for lunar launch/landing infrastructure, which is a critical component of space architecture. It specifically discusses ISRU-based materials (sintering, rock paving, 3D printing) and structural requirements for extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# A review on design and construction of the lunar launch/landing infrastructure

## Abstract
With advances in automated construction and deeper Moon exploration missions, the concept of 'building a house on the moon' is becoming increasingly feasible. The launch/landing (LL) infrastructure plays a vital role in ensuring the safety of landers and astronauts. This review explores LL designs, construction methods (from material production to assembly), and terrestrial testing results. It highlights 3D-printed biomimetic structures as a promising direction.

## 1. Introduction
The safety of launch and landing is key to accomplishing lunar exploration. Unlike Earth, the Moon lacks established facilities, and engine plumes cause damage to the lunar surface through erosion and dust dispersion. Well-constructed LL sites are necessary to ensure safe landings and protect surrounding equipment.

## 2. Design of Lunar Landing/Launching Infrastructure
### 2.1 Launch/Landing Area
Plume simulations reveal differences between Earth and Moon environments. A 40 MT lander can propel particles far; research suggests the LL site diameter should be approximately 100 m (though some proposed sizes range from 20 m to 100 m). 

### 2.2 Launch/Landing Pad
Two main design approaches: 
- **Paving**: Simplest and most cost-effective. Uses moon rocks (layered/graded) or 3D-printed tiles. 
- **3D Extrusion Printing**: Integrated designs like 'The Brute' (using crater slopes as berms) or 'Lunar Pad' (double-layer hollow structures).

### 2.3 Blast Walls and Trenches
Designed to intercept high-speed dust ejecta. Concepts include:
- **Sunflower Vault**: Inward-facing walls inspired by floral buds to deflect flow and capture dust.
- **Eyelashes**: Rows of columns inspired by animal eyelashes to dissipate particle energy.

## 3. In Situ Construction
### 3.1 3D Printing Based on Concrete Extrusion
Involves mixing lunar soil with polymers, sulfur, or geopolymers. 
- **Polyethylene/BP-1 mixture**: Cast at 230°C.
- **Contour Crafting (CCC)**: Capable of building complex structures like the Lunar Pad layers.
- **Sulfur Concrete**: Lighter equipment requirements, integrates mixing and extrusion.

### 3.2 Sintering, Melting, and Assembly
- **LASSS (Large Area Surface Sintering System)**: Rover-based heater for soil stabilization.
- **Solar Concentrator**: Uses Fresnel lenses to reach 1350°C for melting.
- **Selective Separation Sintering (SSS)**: Uses B-powder (base) and S-powder (separator, e.g., Al2O3) to produce interlocking pavers.
- **Laser Melting**: High-power CO2 lasers for high-strength tiles.

### 3.3 Building with Moon Rocks
Pavement using sorted rocks (Armor stone, second layer, bottom layer). Tests indicate stacking rocks in descending order of size yields the best stability.

## 4. Terrestrial Testing
### 4.1 Building Material Testing
| Material | Compressive Strength (MPa) | Findings |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hawaiian Basalt (Sintered) | 21.48 | Equivalent to Portland concrete |
| Laser melted EAC 1A | 56.19–216.29 | Average 93.94 MPa |
| Solar sintered JSC-2A | 2.49 | Young's modulus 0.21 GPa |
| KOHLS-1 Polymer Concrete | 10.3–12.9 | Higher strength in vacuum |
| Geopolymer (Lunamer) | 16.6–33.1 | Potential radiation shielding |

### 4.2 Structural Component Testing
- **Plate Load Tests**: Polymer-concrete pads reached 2.7 MPa compressive strength.
- **Cold Thruster Tests**: KSC (2010) verified erosion protection of stone pavements.
- **Hot Fire Tests**: ICON and NASA verified 'Olympus' designs; Gabion cages remained intact during rocket plume exposure.

## 5. Challenges and Future Directions
1. **Multiphysics Modeling**: Difficulty simulating near-field and far-field plume-particle interactions.
2. **Cracking**: Large-scale 3D printing faces shrinkage and stress concentration issues.
3. **Terrestrial Simulation**: Hard to simulate combined vacuum, low gravity, and high thermal loads simultaneously.
papers.keyFindings: The review identifies three primary construction methods for lunar landing pads: 3D concrete printing (extrusion), sintering/melting, and rock paving/assembly. Laser-melted lunar simulants show the highest compressive strengths (up to 216 MPa), while bionic-inspired structures like the 'Sunflower Vault' offer innovative ways to manage lunar dust ejecta.
papers.findings: The paper provides a structured comparison of LL site design, construction methods, and material testing results. It highlights that ISRU-based methods (particularly laser melting and selective separation sintering) produce materials with structural properties exceeding standard terrestrial concrete. It also emphasizes the importance of zonal design for launch pads to mitigate plume-surface interaction effects.
papers.result: Specific data highlights include a recommended landing site diameter of 100m; laser-melted simulant strengths averaging 93.94 MPa; and experimental verification that sintered basalt tiles (HHQ+) can reach compressive strengths 10 times greater than standard basalt (212.53 MPa).

papers.title: Construction Technology of the Curved ‘Mars House’ Digital Hotel Project in China Using Concrete 3D Printing
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a detailed technical case study on the 3D printing and structural design of a Mars habitat simulation ('Mars House'). It covers concrete 3D printing processes, material performance, and assembly techniques directly relevant to extraterrestrial base construction and additive manufacturing in extreme environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# Construction Technology of the Curved ‘Mars House’ Digital Hotel Project in China Using Concrete 3D Printing

**Authors:** Xiqiang Lin, Hailong Wang, Xiaoyan Sun, Wael Kikhia, Zhennan Wu, Lu Lan, and Jiawei Zhao  
**Publication:** 3DcP 2023, STAM, pp. 241–250, 2024.  
**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-64269-2_30

## Abstract
3D printing technology is a critical candidate for building extraterrestrial bases. This paper introduces the 'Mars House' project in Gansu Province, China, which simulates a Martian landscape. The project involved constructing five egg-shaped buildings using concrete 3D printing. Each house stands 9 m high with a floor area of 46 m², featuring two floors, triangular windows, and a skylight. Components were 3D printed in a factory and assembled on-site in the Gobi Desert. The project demonstrates a safe, fast, and accurate construction process for large-scale curved 3D printed structures.

## 1. Introduction
Research into Martian geology and climate has progressed significantly, sparking interest in long-term human habitation. Construction on Mars presents challenges such as extreme environments and resource limitations, necessitating innovative methods like in-situ resource utilization (ISRU). Various global initiatives (NASA's 3D-Printed Habitat Challenge, ESA's Moon Village) are exploring 3D printing for lunar and Martian habitats. The 'Mars-1 Base' in Jinchang, China, specifically utilizes concrete 3D printing to reduce costs and time while testing architectural designs suitable for Mars.

## 2. Architectural and Structural Design
The egg-shaped form was chosen for its ability to withstand Martian conditions (strong winds, radiation, high-temperature differences) and its ease of fabrication via 3D printing. 
- **Dimensions:** 9 m height, 46 m² floor area.
- **Layout:** Two floors, triangular windows, top skylight.
- **Structure:** Concrete 3D printed outer shell (permanent mould) with an inner core of C40 self-compacting concrete and steel reinforcement.
- **Reinforcement:** 
  - Horizontal steel mesh (every 25 cm).
  - Vertical ϕ12 mm reinforcement inserted into the shell.
  - Structural columns integrated into the ring-shaped modules.

## 3. 3D Printing Construction Technology
### 3.1 Printing Process
The process includes model refinement, factory-based printing of components, embedding lifting points, transportation (1800 km to the site), and on-site assembly.

### 3.2 Equipment and Parameters
- **Printers:** 2 industrial-grade concrete 3D printers.
- **Dimensions:** 14.8 m × 8.6 m × 5.5 m.
- **Layer Height:** 15 mm.
- **Print Speed:** 120 mm/s.
- **Extrusion Width:** 40 mm.
- **Material Volume:** 10.6 m³ of 3D printing material and 17.76 m³ of filling concrete per house.

### 3.3 Concrete Mix and Performance
| Raw Materials | Mix Proportion (%) |
| :--- | :--- |
| OPC42.5 Cement | 38 |
| Artificial Sand | 42 |
| Admixture | 4 |
| Water | 16 |

**Performance Data:**
- **Workability:** 200 mm.
- **Setting Time:** 41 min.
- **Compressive Strength (Cast):** 47.9 MPa.
- **Compressive Strength (Printed):** 40.3 MPa (16% reduction).
- **Interlayer Tensile Strength:** 3.2 MPa.
- **Permeability Grade:** P12.
- **Shrinkage (56 days):** 258 × 10⁻⁶.

### 3.4 Prefabrication and Assembly
Components were printed as annular rings. It took 6 days to print 4 ring-shaped structures for one house using two printers. A 3D printing interface agent was used to ensure bonding between cold joints.

## 4. On-Site Assembly
The printed wall components were transported by truck and assembled using a crane. The assembly of the four main ring-shaped structures took one day. After assembly, vertical reinforcement was welded, and mortar was poured into structural columns to ensure stability.

## 5. Summary
The project successfully demonstrated that factory-prefabricated 3D printed components can significantly shorten construction periods and improve quality control for complex, irregular geometries. It represents one of the largest egg-shaped 3D-printed structures and provides technical experience for future additive intelligent construction in extreme environments.
papers.keyFindings: The study demonstrates the feasibility of constructing large-scale (9m tall) egg-shaped habitats using a hybrid of concrete 3D printing and prefabricated assembly, achieving a compressive strength of 40.3 MPa for printed components. The factory-printing and on-site assembly method reduced construction time significantly, with the main structure assembly taking only one day. The research highlights that the egg-shaped geometry combined with internal reinforcement and self-compacting concrete infill provides a viable structural solution for extreme environment housing.
papers.findings: Development of a full-scale 3D printed habitat simulation using factory-prefabricated annular modules. The research identifies that while printed concrete suffers a 16% strength reduction compared to cast samples, it maintains sufficient mechanical properties (40.3 MPa compressive strength) for structural applications when reinforced with vertical and horizontal steel members.
papers.result: Completed construction of five 9m tall, 46m² habitats in the Gobi Desert; concrete mix achieved 3.2 MPa interlayer tensile strength and P12 permeability; printing parameters established at 120 mm/s speed and 15 mm layer height.

papers.title: Micromechanical testing and property upscaling of planetary rocks: A critical review
papers.relevanceReason: The paper provides a comprehensive review of micromechanical characterization techniques (nanoindentation, AFM) and upscaling methods (AGBM, Mori-Tanaka) for planetary materials, which are critical for ISRU, geotechnical analysis of landing sites, and the construction of extraterrestrial habitats on the Moon, Mars, and asteroids.
papers.markdownContent:
# Micromechanical testing and property upscaling of planetary rocks: A critical review

## 1. Introduction
Understanding the mechanical properties of planetary rocks is crucial for deep space exploration, including tasks like sampling, establishing research stations, and future space mining. These properties influence in-situ resource utilization (ISRU) strategies, material selection, and processing methods.

## 2. Methods of Mineralogical and Microstructural Analyses
Microstructure and mineralogy profoundly influence rock behavior. Techniques discussed include:
- **Microscopy:** SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy) and TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy) for high-resolution imaging of surface topography and internal structures.
- **EDS (Energy-dispersive spectroscopy):** Elemental composition mapping.
- **XCT (X-ray computed tomography):** Non-destructive 3D visualization of internal structures, useful for porosity and density assessment.
- **XRD (X-ray diffraction):** Determining crystalline structure and mineral abundance.
- **EPMA (Electron probe X-ray microanalysis):** High-precision chemical composition analysis.

## 3. Nondestructive Microscale Mechanical Experiments
### 3.1. Nanoindentation
Measures hardness (H) and elastic modulus (E) at the micro/sub-micro scale. It is particularly beneficial for scarce planetary samples as it allows multiple measurements on small fragments without destroying them.
### 3.2. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)
Provides nanoscale resolution for topography and mechanical properties. Modes include contact, non-contact, and tapping. PeakForce QNM allows for quantitative nanomechanical mapping.

## 4. Upscaling of Microscale Mechanical Properties
To relate micro-scale data to the strength of an intact planetary body, various schemes are used:
- **Mori-Tanaka (M-T) scheme:** Considers interaction among inclusions in a matrix.
- **Self-consistent scheme (SCS):** Accounts for stress and strain inhomogeneity in multi-phase composites.
- **Voigt-Reuss-Hill average:** Simple arithmetic/harmonic average of individual constituent moduli.
- **Accurate Grain-Based Modeling (AGBM):** Incorporates real microstructure from TIMA analysis into finite element models to predict macroscale behavior.

## 5. Prospective Applications
- **Sample Return Missions:** Guiding the design of drilling and coring equipment.
- **Planetary Defense:** Predicting the response of asteroids to kinetic impacts or deflection strategies.
- **Extraterrestrial Construction:** Assessing regolith-based construction materials and ensuring structural reliability in extreme environments. 3D printing and laser sintering are highlighted as promising ISRU techniques.

## 6. Conclusions
Microscale testing is essential due to sample scarcity. Combined with advanced upscaling models like AGBM, researchers can generate accurate mechanical data for planetary exploration and construction planning.
papers.keyFindings: Micromechanical characterization via nanoindentation and AFM is vital for extracting physical properties from small, irregular planetary samples. Upscaling schemes like Accurate Grain-Based Modeling (AGBM) successfully bridge micro-measurements to macro-scale structural strength, providing essential data for ISRU and extraterrestrial habitat design.
papers.findings: Nondestructive micromechanical testing provides precise mineralogical and mechanical data for planetary rocks. Upscaling methods effectively link these micro-scale findings to bulk material properties, essential for space engineering and construction applications.
papers.result: The review summarizes elastic modulus and hardness for various meteorites (e.g., Aba Panu: Olivine E=202.7 GPa, H=16.4 GPa) and simulants (e.g., MGS-1 matrix: 18.83 GPa). It demonstrates that the AGBM upscaling method reduces prediction error to 13.21%, compared to 85.57% for the Voigt-Reuss-Hill method.

papers.title: 3D Printing of Tubular Connectors for Space Frame Structures
papers.relevanceReason: The paper discusses Wire-Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM) of steel tubular connectors for space frame structures, which is highly relevant to automated construction and structural design of habitats in space environments.
papers.markdownContent:
# 3D Printing of Tubular Connectors for Space Frame Structures

**Authors:** Ashok Kumar Perka, Bijoy Rajak, and Shibashis Ghosh  
**Affiliations:**  
1. Materials Welding and Joining Research Group, R&D, Tata Steel, Jamshedpur 831001, India  
2. Design & Manufacturing, Altair, Pune 411001, India

## Abstract
In the construction industry, wire-based steel 3D printing (WBS-3DP) technology for structural parts has yet to be explored. This research aims to discuss the application of WAAM in the fabrication of space frame structures for T-K-Y joints with structural integrity. A space frame structure was designed and developed with multi-branch tube connectors using WBS-3DP technology. These tube connectors accommodate steel tubes at arbitrary angles, ensuring smooth and lightweight joints. The prototype demonstrates how WAAM can produce efficient structures by eliminating challenges in tube joining and welding. It was concluded that WBS-3DP has the potential to revolutionize construction through innovative parts and improved digital construction techniques.

## 1 Introduction
Additive Manufacturing (AM), or 3D printing, is a layer-based fabrication process. While industries like aerospace and automotive have embraced metal 3D printing, the construction industry has moved slower due to part size and load requirements. Steel tubes are frequently used for trusses and space frames, but T, K, and Y connections present significant welding and inspection challenges. Conventional fabrication involves complex profile cutting and manual welding, which often results in stress concentrations and requires high skill levels.

## 2 Materials and Methods

### 2.1 Prototype for Tubular Joints Design
A prototype spaceframe model was designed to accommodate various T, K, and Y-type connections (Fig. 3). The design includes tubes of 114 mm diameter (5.4 mm thickness) for the lower part and 76 mm diameter (4.5 mm thickness) for the upper part. Topology optimization (Altair Inspire Print3D) was used to reduce material usage and print time while ensuring structural load distribution.

### 2.2 Tubular Joining Using Additive Manufacturing
Printing was conducted at the Centre for Advanced Welding and Joining (CAWJ), Tata Steel. The system consists of a robotic WAAM setup using Metal Inert Gas (MIG) welding connected to a 6-axis robotic arm.

### 2.3 Printing Process
The 3D CAD model was sliced into layers, and tool paths were generated using Autodesk PowerMill. A carbon steel wire (0.8 mm diameter) was used as feedstock with a shielding gas of 82% Ar and 18% CO2. Bead height was 2 mm, width was 6 mm, and interpass temperature was kept below 120 °C.

**Table 1. Properties of the filler wires used**
| Wire type | Dia. (mm) | C | Si | Mn | Ys (MPa) | UTS (MPa) | %El |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Low carbon steel | 0.8 | 0.07 | 0.83 | 1.48 | >470 | >565 | >22 |

## 3 Results and Discussion

**Table 2. Details of the T-K-Y connections after topology optimisation.**
| Connection | Quantity | Part boundary dimensions (mm) | Weight (kg) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | 2 | 240 x 284 x 340 | 42 |
| B | 4 | 257 x 227 x 338 | 42 |
| C | 2 | 200 x 344 x 304 | 32 |
| D | 3 | 327 x 200 x 224 | 26 |
| E | 6 | 150 x 150 x 201 | 10 |
| F | 3 | 305 x 120 x 100 | 5.5 |

Mechanical characterization showed that the tensile properties were isotropic. Yield strength ranged between 395-405 MPa, and tensile strength between 497-507 MPa. Elongation was between 32-40%. Repeatability of the WAAM procedure was approximately 99% based on the weight of the F-connectors (6.93 - 6.97 kg).

## 4 Conclusions
